query_text: In V-CIP, what are the technical security requirements are explicitly specified for the infrastructure/application?
answer_text:
## Explicit technical security requirements for V-CIP infrastructure / application

The bank opting to undertake V-CIP “shall adhere to the following minimum standards” for “V-CIP Infrastructure” and related technical controls. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]

### 1) Cyber / IT governance, hosting, network domain, outsourcing and cloud controls
- The bank “shall have complied with the RBI guidelines on minimum baseline cyber security and resilience framework for banks, as updated from time to time as well as other general guidelines on IT risks.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
- The bank “shall house the technology infrastructure in its own premises” and “the V-CIP connection and interaction shall necessarily originate from its own secured network domain.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
- “Any technology related outsourcing for the process shall comply with relevant RBI guidelines.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
- Where a cloud deployment model is used, the bank must ensure:
  - “ownership of data in such model rests with the bank only” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
  - “all the data including video recording is transferred to the bank’s exclusively owned / leased server(s) including cloud server, if any, immediately after the V-CIP process is completed” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
  - the “cloud service provider or third-party technology provider assisting the V-CIP shall retain no data.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]

### 2) Encryption and consent integrity
- The bank “shall ensure end-to-end encryption of data between customer device and the hosting point of the V-CIP application, as per appropriate encryption standards.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
- The bank “shall record the customer consent in an auditable and alteration-proof manner.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]

### 3) Geo/IP restriction controls
- The “V-CIP infrastructure / application shall be capable of preventing connection from IP addresses outside India or from spoofed IP addresses.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]

### 4) Video-record security attributes and evidentiary quality
- “The video recordings shall contain the live GPS co-ordinates (geo-tagging) of the customer undertaking the V-CIP and date and time stamp.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
- “The quality of the live video in the V-CIP shall be adequate to allow identification of the customer beyond doubt.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]

### 5) Liveness/spoof detection, face matching and (optional) AI robustness
- The V-CIP application “shall have components with face liveness / spoof detection as well as face matching technology with high degree of accuracy” (while noting that “the ultimate responsibility of any customer identification rests with the bank”). [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
- “The bank may use appropriate artificial intelligence (AI) technology to ensure that the V-CIP is robust.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]

### 6) Security maintenance, event reporting, and mandated security testing
- The bank “shall regularly update the technology infrastructure including application software as well as workflows” based on experience of “detected / attempted / ‘near-miss’ cases of forged identity”. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]
- The bank “shall report any detected case of forged identity through V-CIP as a cyber event under extant regulatory guidelines.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]
- The bank “shall subject the V-CIP infrastructure to necessary tests such as Vulnerability Assessment, Penetration testing and a Security Audit to ensure its robustness and end-to-end encryption capabilities.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]
  - “The bank shall mitigate any critical gap reported under this process before rolling out its implementation.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]
  - “The empanelled auditors of Indian Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT-In) shall conduct such tests.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]
  - Tests “shall also be carried out periodically in conformance to internal / regulatory guidelines.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]
- The bank “shall subject the V-CIP application software and relevant APIs / webservices to appropriate testing of functional, performance, and maintenance strength before being used in live environment.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]
  - The bank “shall roll out the application only after closure of any critical gap found during such tests.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]
  - Such tests “shall also be carried out periodically in conformity with internal / regulatory guidelines.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]

### 7) Technical records/data management controls (storage and audit trail)
- The bank “shall store the entire data and recordings of V-CIP in a system / systems located in India.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.34]
- The bank “shall ensure that the video recording is stored in a safe and secure manner and bears the date and time stamp that affords easy historical data search.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.34]
- The bank “shall preserve the activity log along with the credentials of the official performing the V-CIP.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.34]

### (Contextual definition relevant to security characteristics)
- V-CIP is defined as involving a “seamless, secure, live, informed-consent based audio-visual interaction… through independent verification and by maintaining an audit trail of the process.” [C. Definitions, p.13]

## What is not explicitly specified in the provided sections
- The provided extracts do **not** specify named encryption algorithms/bit-lengths or detailed cryptographic standards beyond “as per appropriate encryption standards.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
- The provided extracts do **not** specify numeric/video resolution or bandwidth parameters, only that quality must be “adequate to allow identification… beyond doubt.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
verification_notes:
Most explicit statements in the main answer are directly supported by the cited source excerpts, primarily [0046] (V-CIP Infrastructure requirements), [0047] (AI option, updates, reporting, testing requirements), and [0049] (records/data management). Citations to the V-CIP definition are supported by [0037]. The 'not explicitly specified' section is consistent with the provided excerpts (no named algorithms/bit-lengths; no numeric video resolution). Main gaps relate to omitted, explicitly stated V-CIP process controls with technical/security implications (e.g., single video file handling on disruption, real-time indicators, prompting rejection, Aadhaar redaction). Inference #1, #3, and #4 are logically entailed by the cited text and appropriately labeled as inferred; inference #2 is directionally reasonable but overreaches by asserting 'contractual' mechanisms as a necessity.

Chapter I – Preliminary
A. Short Title and Commencement.
1. These Directions shall be called the Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks –
Know Your Customer) Directions, 2025.
2. These directions shall come into effect on the day they are placed on the official
website of the RBI.
B. Applicability
3. These directions shall be applicable to Commercial Banks (hereinafter collectively
referred to as 'banks' and individually as a 'bank').
For the purpose of these Directions, ‘Commercial Banks’ mean banking companies
(other than Small Finance Banks, Payment Banks, and Local Area Banks),
corresponding new banks, and the State Bank of India, as defined respectively under
clauses (c), (da), and (nc) of Section 5 of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949.
4. These directions shall also apply to those branches and majority-owned subsidiaries
of the bank which are located abroad, to the extent they are not contradictory to the
local laws in the host country, provided that:
(1) where applicable laws and regulations prohibit implementation of these guidelines,
the bank shall bring the same to the notice of the RBI. The RBI may advise the bank
to take further necessary action, including application of additional measures to
manage the ML / TF risks.
(2) in case there is a variance in KYC / AML standards prescribed by the RBI and the
host country regulators, branches / subsidiaries of the bank shall adopt the more
stringent regulation of the two.
(3) branches/ subsidiaries of a Foreign Incorporated bank may adopt the more
stringent regulation of the two i.e., standards prescribed by RBI and their home country
regulators.
Provided that this rule shall not apply to ‘small accounts’ referred to in paragraph 28 of
Chapter VI.
C. Definitions
5. In these Directions, unless the context otherwise requires, the following meanings

27. The bank opting to undertake V-CIP, shall adhere to the following minimum
standards:
(1) V-CIP Infrastructure
(i) The bank shall have complied with the RBI guidelines on minimum baseline cyber
security and resilience framework for banks, as updated from time to time as well as
other general guidelines on IT risks. The bank shall house the technology infrastructure
in its own premises and the V-CIP connection and interaction shall necessarily
originate from its own secured network domain. Any technology related outsourcing for
the process shall comply with relevant RBI guidelines. Where the bank uses a cloud
deployment model, it shall ensure that ownership of data in such model rests with the
bank only and all the data including video recording is transferred to the bank’s
exclusively owned / leased server(s) including cloud server, if any, immediately after
the V-CIP process is completed and the cloud service provider or third-party
technology provider assisting the V-CIP shall retain no data.
(ii) The bank shall ensure end-to-end encryption of data between customer device and
the hosting point of the V-CIP application, as per appropriate encryption standards.
The bank shall record the customer consent in an auditable and alteration-proof
manner.
(iii) The V-CIP infrastructure / application shall be capable of preventing connection
from IP addresses outside India or from spoofed IP addresses.
(iv) The video recordings shall contain the live GPS co-ordinates (geo-tagging) of the
customer undertaking the V-CIP and date and time stamp. The quality of the live video
in the V-CIP shall be adequate to allow identification of the customer beyond doubt.
(v) The application shall have components with face liveness / spoof detection as well
as face matching technology with high degree of accuracy, even though the ultimate
responsibility of any customer identification rests with the bank.
Explanation: Making specific facial gestures, like blinking of eyes, smiling, frowning,

(4) The aggregate of all credits in a financial year, in all the deposit accounts taken
together, shall not exceed Rupees Two Lakh.
(5) As regards borrowal accounts, the bank shall sanction only term loans. The
aggregate amount of term loans sanctioned shall not exceed ₹60,000 in a year.
(6) The bank shall not allow accounts, both deposit and borrowal, opened using OTP
based e-KYC to operate for more than one year unless it carries out identification as
per paragraph 23 or as per paragraphs 26 and 27 (V-CIP). If the bank uses Aadhaar
details under paragraphs 26 and 27, it shall follow the process in its entirety, including
fresh Aadhaar OTP authentication.
(7) If the bank does not complete the CDD procedure as mentioned above within a
year; (a) in respect of deposit accounts, the bank shall close the same immediately,
and (b) in respect of borrowal accounts, the bank shall allow no further debits.
(8) The bank shall obtain declaration from the customer to the effect that no other
account has been opened nor will be opened using OTP based KYC in non-face-toface mode with any other RE. Further, while uploading KYC information to CKYCR,
bank shall clearly indicate that such accounts are opened using OTP based e-KYC
and other REs shall not open accounts based on the KYC information of accounts
opened with OTP based e-KYC procedure in non-face- to-face mode.
(9) The bank shall have strict monitoring procedures including systems to generate
alerts in case of any non-compliance / violation, to ensure compliance with the abovementioned conditions.
26. The bank may undertake V-CIP to carry out:
(1) CDD in case of new customer onboarding for individual customers, proprietor in
case of proprietorship firm, authorised signatories and Beneficial Owners (BOs) in case
of Legal Entity (LE) customers.
Provided that in case of CDD of a proprietorship firm, the bank shall also obtain the
equivalent e-document of the activity proofs with respect to the proprietorship firm, as
mentioned in paragraph 31 and paragraph 32, apart from undertaking CDD of the
proprietor.

(i) Each bank shall formulate a clear workflow and standard operating procedure for VCIP and ensure adherence to it. The V-CIP process shall be operated only by officials

The bank shall ensure that it also confirms the economic and financial profile /

(xi) The bank shall capture a clear image of PAN card displayed by the customer during
the process, except in cases where e-PAN is provided by the customer. The bank shall

(xii) The use of printed copy of equivalent e-document, including an e-PAN is not valid

(xiii) The authorised official of the bank shall ensure that photograph of the customer

the details provided by the customer.
(xiv) The bank shall permit assisted V-CIP when it takes help of Business
Correspondents (BCs) to facilitate the process only at the customer end. The bank
shall maintain the details of the BC assisting the customer, where it utilises the services
of BCs. The bank shall have the ultimate responsibility for customer due diligence.
(xv) The bank shall make all accounts opened through V-CIP operational only after
subjecting them to concurrent audit to ensure the integrity of process and its
acceptability of its outcome.
(xvi) The bank shall appropriately comply with all matters not specified under the
paragraph but required under other statutes such as the Information Technology (IT)
Act.
(3) V-CIP Records and Data Management
(i) The bank shall store the entire data and recordings of V-CIP in a system / systems
located in India. The bank shall ensure that the video recording is stored in a safe and
secure manner and bears the date and time stamp that affords easy historical data
search. The extant instructions on record management, as stipulated in this direction,
shall also apply to V-CIP.
(ii) The bank shall preserve the activity log along with the credentials of the official
performing the V-CIP.

(v) The bank shall factor in the fact that the V-CIP customer is an existing or new

(vi) The authorised official of the bank performing the V-CIP shall record audio and
video as well as capture a photograph of the customer present for identification and
obtain the identification information using any one of the following:
(a) OTP based Aadhaar e-KYC authentication.
(b) Offline Verification of Aadhaar for identification.
(c) KYC records downloaded from CKYCR, in accordance with paragraph 65, using
the KYC identifier provided by the customer.
(d) Equivalent e-document of Officially Valid Documents (OVDs) including documents
issued through DigiLocker.
(vii) The bank shall ensure to redact or blackout the Aadhaar number in terms of
paragraph 23.
(viii) In case of offline verification of Aadhaar using XML file or Aadhaar Secure QR
Code, the bank shall ensure that the XML file or QR code generation date is not older
than three working days from the date of carrying out V-CIP.
(ix) Further, in line with the prescribed period of three working days for usage of
Aadhaar XML file / Aadhaar QR code, the bank shall ensure that it undertakes video
process of the V-CIP within three working days of downloading / obtaining the
identification information through CKYCR / Aadhaar authentication / equivalent edocument, if in the rare cases, the entire process cannot be completed at one go or
seamlessly. However, the bank shall ensure that no incremental risk is added due to
this.
(x) If the address of the customer is different from that indicated in the OVD, the bank
shall capture suitable records of the current address, as per the existing requirement.

retrieved_sections.title: C. Definitions (p.13)
retrieved_sections.text:
it is incorporated and licensed, and which is unaffiliated with a regulated financial group
that is subject to effective consolidated supervision. Physical presence means
meaningful mind and management located within a country. The existence simply of a
local agent or low-level staff does not constitute physical presence.
(xvi) ‘Video based Customer Identification Process (V-CIP)’: an alternative method
by which an authorised official of the bank conducts customer identification with facial
recognition and customer due diligence. This process involves a seamless, secure,
live, informed- consent based audio-visual interaction with the customer to obtain
identification information required for CDD purpose, and to ascertain the veracity of the
information which the customer furnished, through independent verification and by
maintaining an audit trail of the process. The bank shall treat such processes
complying with prescribed standards and procedures on par with face-to-face CIP for
the purpose of this Direction.
(xvii) ‘Wire transfer’ related definitions:
(a) Batch transfer: A batch transfer is a transfer comprised of a number of individual
wire transfers that are being sent to the same financial institutions but may / may not
be ultimately intended for different persons.
(b) Beneficiary: Beneficiary refers to a natural or legal person or legal arrangement
whom / which the originator identifies as the receiver of the requested wire transfer.
(c) Beneficiary RE: It refers to a financial institution that RBI regulates, which receives
the wire transfer from the ordering financial institution directly or through an
intermediary RE and makes the funds available to the beneficiary.
(d) Cover Payment: Cover Payment refers to a wire transfer that combines a payment
message which the ordering financial institution sends directly to the beneficiary
financial institution with the routing of the funding instruction (the cover) from the
ordering financial institution to the beneficiary financial institution through one or more
intermediary financial institutions.
(e) Cross-border wire transfer: Cross-border wire transfer refers to any wire transfer
where the ordering financial institution and beneficiary financial institution are located
in different countries. This term also refers to any chain of wire transfer in which at least
one of the financial institutions involved is located in a different country.

inferred_points.point: V-CIP sessions are required to be technically geo-fenced such that connections must be blocked if the customer-side IP address is outside India or is spoofed, implying the bank must implement controls to detect and prevent such connections at the application/infrastructure layer.
supporting_definitions: “The V-CIP infrastructure / application shall be capable of preventing connection from IP addresses outside India or from spoofed IP addresses.”
inferred_points.reasoning: The regulation mandates capability to prevent connections from non-India IPs and spoofed IPs. This logically entails implementing technical detection and blocking controls as part of the V-CIP infrastructure/application to enforce the prevention requirement.

inferred_points.point: If the bank uses a cloud deployment model for V-CIP, it must implement contractual and technical mechanisms that ensure third-party/cloud providers cannot retain any V-CIP data after completion, because immediate transfer to bank-owned/leased servers and “retain no data” are mandatory outcomes.
supporting_definitions: “Where the bank uses a cloud deployment model, it shall ensure that ownership of data in such model rests with the bank only and all the data including video recording is transferred to the bank’s exclusively owned / leased server(s) including cloud server, if any, immediately after the V-CIP process is completed and the cloud service provider or third-party technology provider assisting the V-CIP shall retain no data.”
inferred_points.reasoning: The text prescribes strict end-state conditions for cloud use: bank-only data ownership, immediate transfer to bank-owned/leased servers, and zero retention by providers. Achieving these conditions necessarily requires enforceable technical/process/contractual measures; otherwise the mandated outcome cannot be assured.

inferred_points.point: V-CIP technology cannot be rolled into production unless critical findings from security testing (VA/PT/security audit) and application/API testing are remediated, because the regulation conditions rollout on closure/mitigation of “any critical gap”.
supporting_definitions: “The bank shall mitigate any critical gap reported under this process before rolling out its implementation.”
“The bank shall roll out the application only after closure of any critical gap found during such tests.”
inferred_points.reasoning: The regulation explicitly makes mitigation/closure of critical gaps a prerequisite for rollout. Therefore, production deployment is logically prohibited while critical gaps remain open.

inferred_points.point: Because V-CIP data and recordings must be stored in systems located in India, cross-border storage of V-CIP recordings/data would be non-compliant under the provided requirements.
supporting_definitions: “The bank shall store the entire data and recordings of V-CIP in a system / systems located in India.”
inferred_points.reasoning: The regulation sets a location constraint (“located in India”) for storage of all V-CIP data and recordings. This logically excludes storage outside India for the covered data/recordings.

query_text: In V-CIP, what are the technical security requirements are explicitly specified for the infrastructure/application?
answer_text:
### V-CIP technical security requirements for the infrastructure / application (explicitly specified)

#### 1) Baseline cyber / IT risk compliance; hosting, network origination, outsourcing, cloud controls
- The bank **“shall have complied with the RBI guidelines on minimum baseline cyber security and resilience framework for banks… as well as other general guidelines on IT risks.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
- The bank **“shall house the technology infrastructure in its own premises”** and the **“V-CIP connection and interaction shall necessarily originate from its own secured network domain.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
- **Outsourcing controls:** **“Any technology related outsourcing for the process shall comply with relevant RBI guidelines.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
- **Cloud deployment controls:** where cloud is used, the bank must ensure **“ownership of data… rests with the bank only”** and **“all the data including video recording is transferred to the bank’s exclusively owned / leased server(s) including cloud server, if any, immediately after the V-CIP process is completed”**; and **“the cloud service provider or third-party technology provider assisting the V-CIP shall retain no data.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]

#### 2) Encryption and consent capture
- The bank must ensure **“end-to-end encryption of data between customer device and the hosting point of the V-CIP application, as per appropriate encryption standards.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
- The bank must **“record the customer consent in an auditable and alteration-proof manner.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]

#### 3) Network access / geofencing-type security
- The **“V-CIP infrastructure / application shall be capable of preventing connection from IP addresses outside India or from spoofed IP addresses.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]

#### 4) Recording integrity attributes (geo-tagging; timestamp) and video quality
- Video recordings **“shall contain the live GPS co-ordinates (geo-tagging) of the customer… and date and time stamp.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
- **“The quality of the live video in the V-CIP shall be adequate to allow identification of the customer beyond doubt.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]

#### 5) Anti-spoofing, liveness, and face matching technology
- The application **“shall have components with face liveness / spoof detection as well as face matching technology with high degree of accuracy.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, pp.31-32]
- RBI clarifies: **“Making specific facial gestures, like blinking of eyes, smiling, frowning, etc. is not mandatory for liveness check.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, pp.31-32]
- The bank **“may use appropriate artificial intelligence (AI) technology to ensure that the V-CIP is robust.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]

#### 6) Patch / update expectations and cyber-event reporting (for forged identity cases)
- The bank must **“regularly update the technology infrastructure including application software as well as workflows”** based on detected/attempted/near-miss forged identity cases. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]
- The bank must **“report any detected case of forged identity through V-CIP as a cyber event under extant regulatory guidelines.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]

#### 7) Security testing requirements (VAPT; security audit; CERT-In empanelled auditors; periodic testing)
- The bank must test the V-CIP infrastructure via **“Vulnerability Assessment, Penetration testing and a Security Audit to ensure its robustness and end-to-end encryption capabilities.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]
- The bank must **“mitigate any critical gap reported under this process before rolling out its implementation.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]
- **Who conducts tests:** **“The empanelled auditors of Indian Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT-In) shall conduct such tests.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]
- **Periodicity:** **“Such tests shall also be carried out periodically in conformance to internal / regulatory guidelines.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]

#### 8) Application/API testing requirements (functional, performance, maintenance; close critical gaps; periodic)
- The bank must test **“the V-CIP application software and relevant APIs / webservices”** for **“functional, performance, and maintenance strength before being used in live environment.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]
- The bank must **“roll out the application only after closure of any critical gap found during such tests.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]
- **Periodicity:** **“Such tests shall also be carried out periodically in conformity with internal / regulatory guidelines.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]

#### 9) Data localization, secure storage, and logging (records/data management)
- The bank must **“store the entire data and recordings of V-CIP in a system / systems located in India.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.34]
- The bank must ensure video is stored **“in a safe and secure manner”** and **“bears the date and time stamp that affords easy historical data search.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.34]
- The bank must **“preserve the activity log along with the credentials of the official performing the V-CIP.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.34]

#### 10) “Secure” and audit-trail characteristics (definition-level requirements for V-CIP)
- V-CIP is defined as involving a **“seamless, secure, live, informed- consent based audio-visual interaction… through independent verification and by maintaining an audit trail of the process.”** [C. Definitions, p.13]

### What is not explicitly specified in the provided text
- The provided sections do **not** specify particular cryptographic algorithms/key lengths, exact “appropriate encryption standards,” detailed secure coding standards, device hardening requirements, or specific SIEM/log-retention durations for V-CIP beyond the requirements quoted above. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, pp.31-32; A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.34]

locate_results.title: A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.34)
locate_results.reason: Adds requirements that relate to secure storage/localisation and audit logging for V-CIP (data/recordings stored in India, preserved activity logs with official credentials, concurrent audit gating before account becomes operational). locate_results.title: A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.33)

retrieved_sections.title: C. Definitions (p.4)
retrieved_sections.text:
Chapter I – Preliminary
A. Short Title and Commencement.
1. These Directions shall be called the Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks –
Know Your Customer) Directions, 2025.
2. These directions shall come into effect on the day they are placed on the official
website of the RBI.
B. Applicability
3. These directions shall be applicable to Commercial Banks (hereinafter collectively
referred to as 'banks' and individually as a 'bank').
For the purpose of these Directions, ‘Commercial Banks’ mean banking companies
(other than Small Finance Banks, Payment Banks, and Local Area Banks),
corresponding new banks, and the State Bank of India, as defined respectively under
clauses (c), (da), and (nc) of Section 5 of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949.
4. These directions shall also apply to those branches and majority-owned subsidiaries
of the bank which are located abroad, to the extent they are not contradictory to the
local laws in the host country, provided that:
(1) where applicable laws and regulations prohibit implementation of these guidelines,
the bank shall bring the same to the notice of the RBI. The RBI may advise the bank
to take further necessary action, including application of additional measures to
manage the ML / TF risks.
(2) in case there is a variance in KYC / AML standards prescribed by the RBI and the
host country regulators, branches / subsidiaries of the bank shall adopt the more
stringent regulation of the two.
(3) branches/ subsidiaries of a Foreign Incorporated bank may adopt the more
stringent regulation of the two i.e., standards prescribed by RBI and their home country
regulators.
Provided that this rule shall not apply to ‘small accounts’ referred to in paragraph 28 of
Chapter VI.
C. Definitions
5. In these Directions, unless the context otherwise requires, the following meanings

(3) Updation / Periodic updation of KYC for eligible customers.
27. The bank opting to undertake V-CIP, shall adhere to the following minimum
standards:
(1) V-CIP Infrastructure
(i) The bank shall have complied with the RBI guidelines on minimum baseline cyber
security and resilience framework for banks, as updated from time to time as well as
other general guidelines on IT risks. The bank shall house the technology infrastructure
in its own premises and the V-CIP connection and interaction shall necessarily
originate from its own secured network domain. Any technology related outsourcing for
the process shall comply with relevant RBI guidelines. Where the bank uses a cloud
deployment model, it shall ensure that ownership of data in such model rests with the
bank only and all the data including video recording is transferred to the bank’s
exclusively owned / leased server(s) including cloud server, if any, immediately after
the V-CIP process is completed and the cloud service provider or third-party
technology provider assisting the V-CIP shall retain no data.
(ii) The bank shall ensure end-to-end encryption of data between customer device and
the hosting point of the V-CIP application, as per appropriate encryption standards.
The bank shall record the customer consent in an auditable and alteration-proof
manner.
(iii) The V-CIP infrastructure / application shall be capable of preventing connection
from IP addresses outside India or from spoofed IP addresses.
(iv) The video recordings shall contain the live GPS co-ordinates (geo-tagging) of the
customer undertaking the V-CIP and date and time stamp. The quality of the live video
in the V-CIP shall be adequate to allow identification of the customer beyond doubt.
(v) The application shall have components with face liveness / spoof detection as well
as face matching technology with high degree of accuracy, even though the ultimate
responsibility of any customer identification rests with the bank.
Explanation: Making specific facial gestures, like blinking of eyes, smiling, frowning,
etc. is not mandatory for liveness check. The bank shall take due cognizance of special

retrieved_sections.title: A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30)
retrieved_sections.text:
Rupees One Lakh. In case the balance exceeds the threshold, the bank shall cease
the account’s operation, until it completes the CDD as mentioned at (6) below.
(4) The aggregate of all credits in a financial year, in all the deposit accounts taken
together, shall not exceed Rupees Two Lakh.
(5) As regards borrowal accounts, the bank shall sanction only term loans. The
aggregate amount of term loans sanctioned shall not exceed ₹60,000 in a year.
(6) The bank shall not allow accounts, both deposit and borrowal, opened using OTP
based e-KYC to operate for more than one year unless it carries out identification as
per paragraph 23 or as per paragraphs 26 and 27 (V-CIP). If the bank uses Aadhaar
details under paragraphs 26 and 27, it shall follow the process in its entirety, including
fresh Aadhaar OTP authentication.
(7) If the bank does not complete the CDD procedure as mentioned above within a
year; (a) in respect of deposit accounts, the bank shall close the same immediately,
and (b) in respect of borrowal accounts, the bank shall allow no further debits.
(8) The bank shall obtain declaration from the customer to the effect that no other
account has been opened nor will be opened using OTP based KYC in non-face-toface mode with any other RE. Further, while uploading KYC information to CKYCR,
bank shall clearly indicate that such accounts are opened using OTP based e-KYC
and other REs shall not open accounts based on the KYC information of accounts
opened with OTP based e-KYC procedure in non-face- to-face mode.
(9) The bank shall have strict monitoring procedures including systems to generate
alerts in case of any non-compliance / violation, to ensure compliance with the abovementioned conditions.
26. The bank may undertake V-CIP to carry out:
(1) CDD in case of new customer onboarding for individual customers, proprietor in
case of proprietorship firm, authorised signatories and Beneficial Owners (BOs) in case
of Legal Entity (LE) customers.
Provided that in case of CDD of a proprietorship firm, the bank shall also obtain the
equivalent e-document of the activity proofs with respect to the proprietorship firm, as
mentioned in paragraph 31 and paragraph 32, apart from undertaking CDD of the
proprietor.

(vi) The bank may use appropriate artificial intelligence (AI) technology to ensure that
the V-CIP is robust.
(vii) Based on experience of detected / attempted / ‘near-miss’ cases of forged identity,
the bank shall regularly update the technology infrastructure including application
software as well as workflows. The bank shall report any detected case of forged
identity through V-CIP as a cyber event under extant regulatory guidelines.
(viii) The bank shall subject the V-CIP infrastructure to necessary tests such as
Vulnerability Assessment, Penetration testing and a Security Audit to ensure its
robustness and end-to-end encryption capabilities. The bank shall mitigate any critical
gap reported under this process before rolling out its implementation. The empanelled
auditors of Indian Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT-In) shall conduct
such tests. Such tests shall also be carried out periodically in conformance to internal
/ regulatory guidelines.
(ix) The bank shall subject the V-CIP application software and relevant APIs /
webservices to appropriate testing of functional, performance, and maintenance
strength before being used in live environment. The bank shall roll out the application
only after closure of any critical gap found during such tests. Such tests shall also be
carried out periodically in conformity with internal / regulatory guidelines.
(2) V-CIP Procedure
(i) Each bank shall formulate a clear workflow and standard operating procedure for VCIP and ensure adherence to it. The V-CIP process shall be operated only by officials
of the bank specially trained for this purpose. The official shall be capable to carry out
liveness check and detect any other fraudulent manipulation or suspicious conduct of
the customer and act upon it. The liveness check shall not result in exclusion of person
with special needs.
(ii) Disruption of any sort including pausing of video, reconnecting calls, etc., may not
result in creation of multiple video files. If pause or disruption is not leading to the
creation of multiple files, then the bank may not initiate a fresh session. However, in
case of call drop / disconnection, fresh session shall be initiated.
(iii) The bank shall vary the sequence and / or type of questions, including those

retrieved_sections.title: A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.34)
retrieved_sections.text:
The bank shall ensure that it also confirms the economic and financial profile /
information submitted by the customer from the customer undertaking the V-CIP in a
suitable manner.
(xi) The bank shall capture a clear image of PAN card displayed by the customer during
the process, except in cases where e-PAN is provided by the customer. The bank shall
verify the PAN details from the database of the issuing authority ,including through
DigiLocker.
(xii) The use of printed copy of equivalent e-document, including an e-PAN is not valid
for the V-CIP.
(xiii) The authorised official of the bank shall ensure that photograph of the customer
in the Aadhaar / OVD and PAN / e-PAN matches with the customer undertaking the VCIP and the identification details in Aadhaar / OVD and PAN / e-PAN shall match with
the details provided by the customer.
(xiv) The bank shall permit assisted V-CIP when it takes help of Business
Correspondents (BCs) to facilitate the process only at the customer end. The bank
shall maintain the details of the BC assisting the customer, where it utilises the services
of BCs. The bank shall have the ultimate responsibility for customer due diligence.
(xv) The bank shall make all accounts opened through V-CIP operational only after
subjecting them to concurrent audit to ensure the integrity of process and its
acceptability of its outcome.
(xvi) The bank shall appropriately comply with all matters not specified under the
paragraph but required under other statutes such as the Information Technology (IT)
Act.
(3) V-CIP Records and Data Management
(i) The bank shall store the entire data and recordings of V-CIP in a system / systems
located in India. The bank shall ensure that the video recording is stored in a safe and
secure manner and bears the date and time stamp that affords easy historical data
search. The extant instructions on record management, as stipulated in this direction,
shall also apply to V-CIP.
(ii) The bank shall preserve the activity log along with the credentials of the official
performing the V-CIP.

interactions are real-time and not pre-recorded.
(iv) The bank shall reject the account opening process if it observes any prompting at
the customer end.
(v) The bank shall factor in the fact that the V-CIP customer is an existing or new
customer, or if the case relates to one rejected earlier or if the name appears in some
negative list, at an appropriate stage of workflow.
(vi) The authorised official of the bank performing the V-CIP shall record audio and
video as well as capture a photograph of the customer present for identification and
obtain the identification information using any one of the following:
(a) OTP based Aadhaar e-KYC authentication.
(b) Offline Verification of Aadhaar for identification.
(c) KYC records downloaded from CKYCR, in accordance with paragraph 65, using
the KYC identifier provided by the customer.
(d) Equivalent e-document of Officially Valid Documents (OVDs) including documents
issued through DigiLocker.
(vii) The bank shall ensure to redact or blackout the Aadhaar number in terms of
paragraph 23.
(viii) In case of offline verification of Aadhaar using XML file or Aadhaar Secure QR
Code, the bank shall ensure that the XML file or QR code generation date is not older
than three working days from the date of carrying out V-CIP.
(ix) Further, in line with the prescribed period of three working days for usage of
Aadhaar XML file / Aadhaar QR code, the bank shall ensure that it undertakes video
process of the V-CIP within three working days of downloading / obtaining the
identification information through CKYCR / Aadhaar authentication / equivalent edocument, if in the rare cases, the entire process cannot be completed at one go or
seamlessly. However, the bank shall ensure that no incremental risk is added due to
this.
(x) If the address of the customer is different from that indicated in the OVD, the bank
shall capture suitable records of the current address, as per the existing requirement.

retrieved_sections.title: C. Definitions (p.13)
retrieved_sections.text:
it is incorporated and licensed, and which is unaffiliated with a regulated financial group
that is subject to effective consolidated supervision. Physical presence means
meaningful mind and management located within a country. The existence simply of a
local agent or low-level staff does not constitute physical presence.
(xvi) ‘Video based Customer Identification Process (V-CIP)’: an alternative method
by which an authorised official of the bank conducts customer identification with facial
recognition and customer due diligence. This process involves a seamless, secure,
live, informed- consent based audio-visual interaction with the customer to obtain
identification information required for CDD purpose, and to ascertain the veracity of the
information which the customer furnished, through independent verification and by
maintaining an audit trail of the process. The bank shall treat such processes
complying with prescribed standards and procedures on par with face-to-face CIP for
the purpose of this Direction.
(xvii) ‘Wire transfer’ related definitions:
(a) Batch transfer: A batch transfer is a transfer comprised of a number of individual
wire transfers that are being sent to the same financial institutions but may / may not
be ultimately intended for different persons.
(b) Beneficiary: Beneficiary refers to a natural or legal person or legal arrangement
whom / which the originator identifies as the receiver of the requested wire transfer.
(c) Beneficiary RE: It refers to a financial institution that RBI regulates, which receives
the wire transfer from the ordering financial institution directly or through an
intermediary RE and makes the funds available to the beneficiary.
(d) Cover Payment: Cover Payment refers to a wire transfer that combines a payment
message which the ordering financial institution sends directly to the beneficiary
financial institution with the routing of the funding instruction (the cover) from the
ordering financial institution to the beneficiary financial institution through one or more
intermediary financial institutions.
(e) Cross-border wire transfer: Cross-border wire transfer refers to any wire transfer
where the ordering financial institution and beneficiary financial institution are located
in different countries. This term also refers to any chain of wire transfer in which at least
one of the financial institutions involved is located in a different country.

(ix) ‘KYC Templates’ means templates prepared to facilitate collating and reporting
KYC data to the CKYCR, for individuals and legal entities.
(x) ‘Non-face-to-face customers’ means customers who open accounts without
visiting the branch / offices of the bank or meeting the officials of the bank.
(xi) ‘On-going Due Diligence’ means regular monitoring of transactions in accounts
to ensure that transactions are consistent with the bank’s knowledge about the
customers, customers’ business and risk profile, the source of funds / wealth.
(xii) Payable-through accounts: The term payable-through accounts refers to
correspondent accounts that third parties use directly to transact business on their own
behalf.
(xiii) ‘Periodic Updation’ means the steps taken to ensure that documents, data or
information collected under the CDD process are kept up-to-date and relevant by
undertaking reviews of existing records at the periodicity prescribed by the RBI.
(xiv) 'Regulated Entities (REs)’ means:
(a) all Scheduled Commercial Banks (SCBs) / Regional Rural Banks (RRBs) / Local
Area Banks (LABs) / All Primary (Urban) Co-operative Banks (UCBs) / State and
Central Co-operative Banks (StCBs / CCBs), and any other entity which has been
licensed under section 22 of Banking Regulation Act, 1949, which as a group shall be
referred as ‘banks’
(b) All India Financial Institutions (AIFIs)
(c) All Non-Banking Finance Companies (NBFCs), Miscellaneous Non-Banking
Companies (MNBCs) and Residuary Non-Banking Companies (RNBCs)

inferred_points.point: A bank cannot go live with V-CIP unless critical security gaps identified in mandated testing/audit are remediated/closed.
supporting_definitions: “The bank shall mitigate any critical gap reported under this process before rolling out its implementation.”
“The bank shall roll out the application only after closure of any critical gap found during such tests.”
inferred_points.reasoning: The regulation explicitly conditions rollout on remediation/closure of “any critical gap”; therefore, operating V-CIP in production prior to closing such gaps would be non-compliant.

inferred_points.point: If any third party (including a cloud service provider) assists in V-CIP, the bank must ensure the third party retains no V-CIP data after completion; therefore, the permitted role of third parties is constrained to processing/facilitation without post-process data retention.
supporting_definitions: “Where the bank uses a cloud deployment model, it shall ensure that ownership of data in such model rests with the bank only and all the data including video recording is transferred to the bank’s exclusively owned / leased server(s) including cloud server, if any, immediately after the V-CIP process is completed and the cloud service provider or third-party technology provider assisting the V-CIP shall retain no data.”
inferred_points.reasoning: The explicit prohibition on third-party retention, combined with the requirement for immediate transfer to bank-owned/leased servers and bank-only data ownership, logically limits third parties from storing V-CIP artifacts beyond the session completion.

inferred_points.point: V-CIP data localization applies end-to-end for records/recordings: V-CIP artifacts must ultimately reside in India even if cloud is used, because storage is mandated “in India” and cloud (if any) must be bank-owned/leased and receive immediate transfer after completion.
supporting_definitions: “The bank shall store the entire data and recordings of V-CIP in a system / systems located in India.”
“...all the data including video recording is transferred to the bank’s exclusively owned / leased server(s) including cloud server, if any, immediately after the V-CIP process is completed...”
inferred_points.reasoning: The record-management rule mandates India-based storage of the “entire data and recordings.” The infrastructure rule further requires that after V-CIP completion, all data (including video) is transferred to bank-owned/leased servers (including cloud). Read together, this entails that the end-state storage location must be India-based even in a cloud model.

inferred_points.point: The requirement to prevent connections from “IP addresses outside India or from spoofed IP addresses” implies the V-CIP application/infrastructure must implement technical controls for (a) IP geolocation-based blocking and (b) spoofing detection/mitigation mechanisms as part of access control.
supporting_definitions: “The V-CIP infrastructure / application shall be capable of preventing connection from IP addresses outside India or from spoofed IP addresses.”
inferred_points.reasoning: To be “capable of preventing” such connections, the system must implement some method to detect and block non-India IPs and spoofed IPs; while specific technologies are not prescribed, the existence of technical enforcement is logically necessary to meet the capability requirement.

query_text: In V-CIP, what are the technical security requirements are explicitly specified for the infrastructure/application?
sub_queries: Identify all security-related requirements explicitly prescribed for the V-CIP infrastructure (e.g., hosting, network/security controls, audit/logging, access control, data storage/retention).

Identify all security-related requirements explicitly prescribed for the V-CIP application/software (e.g., encryption, secure transmission, integrity controls, authentication/authorization, tamper-proofing, session recording).
Extract any explicit RBI-mandated standards/certifications/assurance requirements applicable to V-CIP systems (if specified).
answer_text:
The V-CIP technical security requirements explicitly specified for the V-CIP infrastructure/application (minimum standards) are as follows:

## 1) Cybersecurity baseline and hosting / network domain controls
- The bank must have “complied with the RBI guidelines on minimum baseline cyber security and resilience framework for banks… as well as other general guidelines on IT risks.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
- The bank must “house the technology infrastructure in its own premises” and the “V-CIP connection and interaction shall necessarily originate from its own secured network domain.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]

## 2) Outsourcing and cloud security / data ownership controls
- “Any technology related outsourcing for the process shall comply with relevant RBI guidelines.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
- If a cloud deployment model is used, the bank must ensure:
  - “ownership of data in such model rests with the bank only”; and [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
  - “all the data including video recording is transferred to the bank’s exclusively owned / leased server(s) including cloud server, if any, immediately after the V-CIP process is completed”; and [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
  - “the cloud service provider or third-party technology provider assisting the V-CIP shall retain no data.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]

## 3) Encryption and consent capture requirements
- The bank must “ensure end-to-end encryption of data between customer device and the hosting point of the V-CIP application, as per appropriate encryption standards.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
- The bank must “record the customer consent in an auditable and alteration-proof manner.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]

## 4) IP / geo-fencing and anti-spoofing controls
- The “V-CIP infrastructure / application shall be capable of preventing connection from IP addresses outside India or from spoofed IP addresses.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]

## 5) Video security metadata and quality requirements
- Video recordings must contain “the live GPS co-ordinates (geo-tagging) of the customer… and date and time stamp.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
- The “quality of the live video… shall be adequate to allow identification of the customer beyond doubt.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]

## 6) Biometrics / anti-fraud technology requirements in the application
- The application must have “components with face liveness / spoof detection as well as face matching technology with high degree of accuracy” (while noting “ultimate responsibility of any customer identification rests with the bank”). [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
- The bank “may use appropriate artificial intelligence (AI) technology to ensure that the V-CIP is robust.” (permissive, not mandatory) [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]

## 7) Patch/upgrade, cyber event reporting and resilience
- The bank must “regularly update the technology infrastructure including application software as well as workflows” based on detected/attempted/near-miss forged identity cases. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]
- The bank must “report any detected case of forged identity through V-CIP as a cyber event under extant regulatory guidelines.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]

## 8) Security testing / assurance requirements (including auditor qualification)
- The bank must subject V-CIP infrastructure to “necessary tests such as Vulnerability Assessment, Penetration testing and a Security Audit to ensure its robustness and end-to-end encryption capabilities,” and “mitigate any critical gap… before rolling out its implementation.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]
- “The empanelled auditors of Indian Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT-In) shall conduct such tests,” and these tests must also be conducted “periodically.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]

## 9) Application/API testing requirements before production
- The bank must subject “V-CIP application software and relevant APIs / webservices” to “appropriate testing of functional, performance, and maintenance strength” before live use, and must “roll out the application only after closure of any critical gap.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]

## 10) Data localisation and secure record storage (V-CIP records/data management)
- The bank must “store the entire data and recordings of V-CIP in a system / systems located in India.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.34]
- The bank must ensure video recording is stored “in a safe and secure manner” and “bears the date and time stamp that affords easy historical data search.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.34]
- The bank must “preserve the activity log along with the credentials of the official performing the V-CIP.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.34]

## What is NOT explicitly specified in the provided text
- The regulation requires end-to-end encryption “as per appropriate encryption standards” but does not specify particular cryptographic algorithms/protocol versions (e.g., TLS version/ciphers). [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
- It requires “high degree of accuracy” for face matching but does not provide numeric accuracy/FAR/FRR thresholds. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
- It mandates blocking “IP addresses outside India” / “spoofed IP addresses” but does not specify the exact technical method (e.g., specific IP intelligence or spoofing-detection technique). [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]

routing_log.query_text: In V-CIP, what are the technical security requirements are explicitly specified for the infrastructure/application?

locate_results.title: A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.31)
locate_results.reason: Core V-CIP clause specifying minimum infrastructure/technical standards: compliance with RBI cyber security/IT risk guidelines; requirement that V-CIP originate from bank’s secured network; secure hosting expectations (bank premises/on-prem); detailed cloud conditions (bank data ownership, immediate transfer to bank-owned/leased servers, zero retention by CSP/third parties); end-to-end encryption; consent capture that is auditable/alteration-proof; IP restrictions (block outside/spoofed India IPs); session/video recording integrity controls including live GPS geo-tagging, date/time stamps, and quality requirements; and anti-impersonation controls such as face liveness/spoof detection and face matching.

locate_results.title: A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.32)
locate_results.reason: Contains explicit security assurance and auditability prerequisites for V-CIP (reporting forged identity as a cyber event, CERT-In empanelled auditor requirement, VA/PT and security audits, remediation of critical gaps before rollout and periodically, and software/API functional/performance testing with closure of critical issues).

locate_results.title: A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.34)
locate_results.reason: Adds explicit operational controls tied to system auditability and record keeping for V-CIP (concurrent audit prerequisite before account becomes operational, storage of V-CIP data/recordings in India with date/time stamps, preservation of activity logs including official credentials; also covers verification via issuing authority sources and constraints on acceptable e-docs).

locate_results.title: Chapter VII – Record Management
locate_results.reason: Sets the explicit record preservation/retention periods applicable to KYC/CIP-related records (including customer identification records and transaction records). This is the most likely place for RBI’s prescribed retention timelines that would apply to V-CIP recordings/logs as part of customer identification/verification records.

locate_results.title: A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.33)
locate_results.reason: Operational/real-time integrity and anti-impersonation safeguards that often translate into technical requirements: liveness checks, handling call drops by requiring a fresh session, varying questions to ensure real-time interaction, rejecting onboarding if prompting is observed, and controls around permissible identification sources and timelines (e.g., Aadhaar XML/Secure QR validity). Useful for clauses addressing session integrity and impersonation risk.

locate_results.title: C. Definitions (p.13)
locate_results.reason: Defines V-CIP and characterises it as a secure, live, consent-based audio-visual interaction with audit trail requirements and equivalence to face-to-face CIP when compliant—useful for interpreting what constitutes mandatory security/auditability attributes of V-CIP.

locate_results.title: B. Other General Guidelines:
locate_results.reason: Sets overarching governance expectations relevant to system auditability/controls (independent evaluation, concurrent/internal audit arrangements, quarterly audit notes to Audit Committee, and non-outsourcing of KYC decision-making), which may be referenced when framing V-CIP control environment.

retrieved_sections.title: C. Definitions (p.4)
retrieved_sections.text:
Chapter I – Preliminary
A. Short Title and Commencement.
1. These Directions shall be called the Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks –
Know Your Customer) Directions, 2025.
2. These directions shall come into effect on the day they are placed on the official
website of the RBI.
B. Applicability
3. These directions shall be applicable to Commercial Banks (hereinafter collectively
referred to as 'banks' and individually as a 'bank').
For the purpose of these Directions, ‘Commercial Banks’ mean banking companies
(other than Small Finance Banks, Payment Banks, and Local Area Banks),
corresponding new banks, and the State Bank of India, as defined respectively under
clauses (c), (da), and (nc) of Section 5 of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949.
4. These directions shall also apply to those branches and majority-owned subsidiaries
of the bank which are located abroad, to the extent they are not contradictory to the
local laws in the host country, provided that:
(1) where applicable laws and regulations prohibit implementation of these guidelines,
the bank shall bring the same to the notice of the RBI. The RBI may advise the bank
to take further necessary action, including application of additional measures to
manage the ML / TF risks.
(2) in case there is a variance in KYC / AML standards prescribed by the RBI and the
host country regulators, branches / subsidiaries of the bank shall adopt the more
stringent regulation of the two.
(3) branches/ subsidiaries of a Foreign Incorporated bank may adopt the more
stringent regulation of the two i.e., standards prescribed by RBI and their home country
regulators.
Provided that this rule shall not apply to ‘small accounts’ referred to in paragraph 28 of
Chapter VI.
C. Definitions
5. In these Directions, unless the context otherwise requires, the following meanings

OTP based e-KYC authentication as per paragraph 25.
(3) Updation / Periodic updation of KYC for eligible customers.
27. The bank opting to undertake V-CIP, shall adhere to the following minimum
standards:
(1) V-CIP Infrastructure
(i) The bank shall have complied with the RBI guidelines on minimum baseline cyber
security and resilience framework for banks, as updated from time to time as well as
other general guidelines on IT risks. The bank shall house the technology infrastructure
in its own premises and the V-CIP connection and interaction shall necessarily
originate from its own secured network domain. Any technology related outsourcing for
the process shall comply with relevant RBI guidelines. Where the bank uses a cloud
deployment model, it shall ensure that ownership of data in such model rests with the
bank only and all the data including video recording is transferred to the bank’s
exclusively owned / leased server(s) including cloud server, if any, immediately after
the V-CIP process is completed and the cloud service provider or third-party
technology provider assisting the V-CIP shall retain no data.
(ii) The bank shall ensure end-to-end encryption of data between customer device and
the hosting point of the V-CIP application, as per appropriate encryption standards.
The bank shall record the customer consent in an auditable and alteration-proof
manner.
(iii) The V-CIP infrastructure / application shall be capable of preventing connection
from IP addresses outside India or from spoofed IP addresses.
(iv) The video recordings shall contain the live GPS co-ordinates (geo-tagging) of the
customer undertaking the V-CIP and date and time stamp. The quality of the live video
in the V-CIP shall be adequate to allow identification of the customer beyond doubt.
(v) The application shall have components with face liveness / spoof detection as well
as face matching technology with high degree of accuracy, even though the ultimate
responsibility of any customer identification rests with the bank.
Explanation: Making specific facial gestures, like blinking of eyes, smiling, frowning,
etc. is not mandatory for liveness check. The bank shall take due cognizance of special

retrieved_sections.title: A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30)
retrieved_sections.text:
Rupees One Lakh. In case the balance exceeds the threshold, the bank shall cease
the account’s operation, until it completes the CDD as mentioned at (6) below.
(4) The aggregate of all credits in a financial year, in all the deposit accounts taken
together, shall not exceed Rupees Two Lakh.
(5) As regards borrowal accounts, the bank shall sanction only term loans. The
aggregate amount of term loans sanctioned shall not exceed ₹60,000 in a year.
(6) The bank shall not allow accounts, both deposit and borrowal, opened using OTP
based e-KYC to operate for more than one year unless it carries out identification as
per paragraph 23 or as per paragraphs 26 and 27 (V-CIP). If the bank uses Aadhaar
details under paragraphs 26 and 27, it shall follow the process in its entirety, including
fresh Aadhaar OTP authentication.
(7) If the bank does not complete the CDD procedure as mentioned above within a
year; (a) in respect of deposit accounts, the bank shall close the same immediately,
and (b) in respect of borrowal accounts, the bank shall allow no further debits.
(8) The bank shall obtain declaration from the customer to the effect that no other
account has been opened nor will be opened using OTP based KYC in non-face-toface mode with any other RE. Further, while uploading KYC information to CKYCR,
bank shall clearly indicate that such accounts are opened using OTP based e-KYC
and other REs shall not open accounts based on the KYC information of accounts
opened with OTP based e-KYC procedure in non-face- to-face mode.
(9) The bank shall have strict monitoring procedures including systems to generate
alerts in case of any non-compliance / violation, to ensure compliance with the abovementioned conditions.
26. The bank may undertake V-CIP to carry out:
(1) CDD in case of new customer onboarding for individual customers, proprietor in
case of proprietorship firm, authorised signatories and Beneficial Owners (BOs) in case
of Legal Entity (LE) customers.
Provided that in case of CDD of a proprietorship firm, the bank shall also obtain the
equivalent e-document of the activity proofs with respect to the proprietorship firm, as
mentioned in paragraph 31 and paragraph 32, apart from undertaking CDD of the
proprietor.

(vi) The bank may use appropriate artificial intelligence (AI) technology to ensure that
the V-CIP is robust.
(vii) Based on experience of detected / attempted / ‘near-miss’ cases of forged identity,
the bank shall regularly update the technology infrastructure including application
software as well as workflows. The bank shall report any detected case of forged
identity through V-CIP as a cyber event under extant regulatory guidelines.
(viii) The bank shall subject the V-CIP infrastructure to necessary tests such as
Vulnerability Assessment, Penetration testing and a Security Audit to ensure its
robustness and end-to-end encryption capabilities. The bank shall mitigate any critical
gap reported under this process before rolling out its implementation. The empanelled
auditors of Indian Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT-In) shall conduct
such tests. Such tests shall also be carried out periodically in conformance to internal
/ regulatory guidelines.
(ix) The bank shall subject the V-CIP application software and relevant APIs /
webservices to appropriate testing of functional, performance, and maintenance
strength before being used in live environment. The bank shall roll out the application
only after closure of any critical gap found during such tests. Such tests shall also be
carried out periodically in conformity with internal / regulatory guidelines.
(2) V-CIP Procedure
(i) Each bank shall formulate a clear workflow and standard operating procedure for VCIP and ensure adherence to it. The V-CIP process shall be operated only by officials
of the bank specially trained for this purpose. The official shall be capable to carry out
liveness check and detect any other fraudulent manipulation or suspicious conduct of
the customer and act upon it. The liveness check shall not result in exclusion of person
with special needs.
(ii) Disruption of any sort including pausing of video, reconnecting calls, etc., may not
result in creation of multiple video files. If pause or disruption is not leading to the
creation of multiple files, then the bank may not initiate a fresh session. However, in
case of call drop / disconnection, fresh session shall be initiated.
(iii) The bank shall vary the sequence and / or type of questions, including those

retrieved_sections.title: A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.34)
retrieved_sections.text:
The bank shall ensure that it also confirms the economic and financial profile /
information submitted by the customer from the customer undertaking the V-CIP in a
suitable manner.
(xi) The bank shall capture a clear image of PAN card displayed by the customer during
the process, except in cases where e-PAN is provided by the customer. The bank shall
verify the PAN details from the database of the issuing authority ,including through
DigiLocker.
(xii) The use of printed copy of equivalent e-document, including an e-PAN is not valid
for the V-CIP.
(xiii) The authorised official of the bank shall ensure that photograph of the customer
in the Aadhaar / OVD and PAN / e-PAN matches with the customer undertaking the VCIP and the identification details in Aadhaar / OVD and PAN / e-PAN shall match with
the details provided by the customer.
(xiv) The bank shall permit assisted V-CIP when it takes help of Business
Correspondents (BCs) to facilitate the process only at the customer end. The bank
shall maintain the details of the BC assisting the customer, where it utilises the services
of BCs. The bank shall have the ultimate responsibility for customer due diligence.
(xv) The bank shall make all accounts opened through V-CIP operational only after
subjecting them to concurrent audit to ensure the integrity of process and its
acceptability of its outcome.
(xvi) The bank shall appropriately comply with all matters not specified under the
paragraph but required under other statutes such as the Information Technology (IT)
Act.
(3) V-CIP Records and Data Management
(i) The bank shall store the entire data and recordings of V-CIP in a system / systems
located in India. The bank shall ensure that the video recording is stored in a safe and
secure manner and bears the date and time stamp that affords easy historical data
search. The extant instructions on record management, as stipulated in this direction,
shall also apply to V-CIP.
(ii) The bank shall preserve the activity log along with the credentials of the official
performing the V-CIP.
