retrieved_sections.text:
it is incorporated and licensed, and which is unaffiliated with a regulated financial group
that is subject to effective consolidated supervision. Physical presence means
meaningful mind and management located within a country. The existence simply of a
local agent or low-level staff does not constitute physical presence.
(xvi) ‘Video based Customer Identification Process (V-CIP)’: an alternative method
by which an authorised official of the bank conducts customer identification with facial
recognition and customer due diligence. This process involves a seamless, secure,
live, informed- consent based audio-visual interaction with the customer to obtain
identification information required for CDD purpose, and to ascertain the veracity of the
information which the customer furnished, through independent verification and by
maintaining an audit trail of the process. The bank shall treat such processes
complying with prescribed standards and procedures on par with face-to-face CIP for
the purpose of this Direction.
(xvii) ‘Wire transfer’ related definitions:
(a) Batch transfer: A batch transfer is a transfer comprised of a number of individual
wire transfers that are being sent to the same financial institutions but may / may not
be ultimately intended for different persons.
(b) Beneficiary: Beneficiary refers to a natural or legal person or legal arrangement
whom / which the originator identifies as the receiver of the requested wire transfer.
(c) Beneficiary RE: It refers to a financial institution that RBI regulates, which receives
the wire transfer from the ordering financial institution directly or through an
intermediary RE and makes the funds available to the beneficiary.
(d) Cover Payment: Cover Payment refers to a wire transfer that combines a payment
message which the ordering financial institution sends directly to the beneficiary
financial institution with the routing of the funding instruction (the cover) from the
ordering financial institution to the beneficiary financial institution through one or more
intermediary financial institutions.
(e) Cross-border wire transfer: Cross-border wire transfer refers to any wire transfer
where the ordering financial institution and beneficiary financial institution are located
in different countries. This term also refers to any chain of wire transfer in which at least
one of the financial institutions involved is located in a different country.

inferred_points.point: The V-CIP application/infrastructure must enforce geo-fencing controls such that customers using IP addresses outside India (or using spoofed IPs) cannot successfully initiate/complete a V-CIP session.
supporting_definitions: “The V-CIP infrastructure / application shall be capable of preventing connection from IP addresses outside India or from spoofed IP addresses.”
inferred_points.reasoning: The regulation mandates technical capability to prevent connections from IPs outside India/spoofed IPs. This logically entails implementing and operating geo-fencing/anti-spoofing network controls in the V-CIP application/infrastructure so such sessions cannot be established or continued.

inferred_points.point: If a bank uses a cloud deployment model for V-CIP, the design must ensure immediate post-session data migration to bank-controlled servers and must prevent the cloud/third-party provider from retaining any V-CIP data (including video).
supporting_definitions: “Where the bank uses a cloud deployment model, it shall ensure that ownership of data in such model rests with the bank only and all the data including video recording is transferred to the bank’s exclusively owned / leased server(s) including cloud server, if any, immediately after the V-CIP process is completed and the cloud service provider or third-party technology provider assisting the V-CIP shall retain no data.”
inferred_points.reasoning: The text specifies both (i) bank-only ownership and (ii) immediate transfer to bank-owned/leased servers and (iii) zero retention by providers. Collectively, this necessarily implies the technical architecture must be built to execute immediate transfer and enforce non-retention controls (contractual plus technical) when cloud/third-party support is used.

inferred_points.point: Prior to go-live, a bank cannot deploy V-CIP until all critical findings from both (a) security testing (VA/PT/Security Audit) and (b) application/API testing have been remediated/closed; therefore, go-live is conditional on closure of critical gaps across these testing tracks.
supporting_definitions: “The bank shall mitigate any critical gap reported under this process before rolling out its implementation.”
“The bank shall roll out the application only after closure of any critical gap found during such tests.”
inferred_points.reasoning: The regulation imposes two separate pre-rollout conditions: mitigation of critical gaps from infrastructure security tests and closure of critical gaps from application/API tests. It follows that V-CIP cannot be rolled out unless critical gaps in either testing stream are addressed.

query_text: In V-CIP, what are the technical security requirements are explicitly specified for the infrastructure/application?
answer_text:
## V-CIP technical security requirements explicitly specified for the infrastructure / application

### 1) Baseline cyber / IT risk compliance and secure hosting
- The bank **“shall have complied with the RBI guidelines on minimum baseline cyber security and resilience framework for banks… as well as other general guidelines on IT risks”**. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
- The bank **“shall house the technology infrastructure in its own premises”** and **“the V-CIP connection and interaction shall necessarily originate from its own secured network domain.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
- Any technology-related outsourcing for V-CIP **“shall comply with relevant RBI guidelines.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]

### 2) Cloud security and data ownership / non-retention by third parties
- If a cloud deployment model is used, the bank must ensure **“ownership of data in such model rests with the bank only”**. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
- The bank must ensure **“all the data including video recording is transferred to the bank’s exclusively owned / leased server(s) including cloud server, if any, immediately after the V-CIP process is completed”**. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
- The **“cloud service provider or third-party technology provider assisting the V-CIP shall retain no data.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]

### 3) Encryption and consent capture
- The bank must ensure **“end-to-end encryption of data between customer device and the hosting point of the V-CIP application, as per appropriate encryption standards.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
- The bank must **“record the customer consent in an auditable and alteration-proof manner.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]

### 4) Network access controls / geo-fencing against foreign or spoofed IPs
- The V-CIP infrastructure / application must be capable of **“preventing connection from IP addresses outside India or from spoofed IP addresses.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]

### 5) Video-record security metadata and quality requirements
- Video recordings must contain **“live GPS co-ordinates (geo-tagging)… and date and time stamp.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
- The bank must ensure **“The quality of the live video… shall be adequate to allow identification of the customer beyond doubt.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]

### 6) Anti-spoofing / biometrics controls
- The application must include **“face liveness / spoof detection as well as face matching technology with high degree of accuracy”** (while noting that responsibility remains with the bank). [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, pp.31-32]
- The bank **“may use appropriate artificial intelligence (AI) technology to ensure that the V-CIP is robust.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]

### 7) Ongoing security maintenance, event reporting, and periodic upgrades
- The bank must **“regularly update the technology infrastructure including application software as well as workflows”** based on detected/attempted/near-miss forged identity cases. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]
- The bank must **“report any detected case of forged identity through V-CIP as a cyber event under extant regulatory guidelines.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]

### 8) Mandatory security testing / audits (VAPT and security audit) with conditions for rollout
- The bank must test V-CIP infrastructure via **“Vulnerability Assessment, Penetration testing and a Security Audit to ensure its robustness and end-to-end encryption capabilities.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]
- It must **“mitigate any critical gap… before rolling out its implementation.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]
- Such tests must be conducted by **“empanelled auditors of Indian Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT-In)”** and **“carried out periodically”** per internal/regulatory guidelines. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]

### 9) Application/API testing prior to production and periodic testing
- The bank must subject **“V-CIP application software and relevant APIs / webservices”** to **“appropriate testing of functional, performance, and maintenance strength”** before live use. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]
- It must **“roll out the application only after closure of any critical gap”** and must also test periodically per internal/regulatory guidelines. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.32]

### 10) Data localization, secure storage, and audit logs (records & data management)
- The bank must store **“the entire data and recordings of V-CIP in a system / systems located in India.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.34]
- The bank must ensure the recording is stored **“in a safe and secure manner”** and **“bears the date and time stamp that affords easy historical data search.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.34]
- The bank must **“preserve the activity log along with the credentials of the official performing the V-CIP.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.34]

### 11) Security characteristics embedded in the definition of V-CIP
- V-CIP is defined as involving a **“seamless, secure, live, informed-consent based audio-visual interaction”** and requires **“maintaining an audit trail of the process.”** [C. Definitions, p.13]

## What is not explicitly specified in the retrieved sections
- The retrieved text requires encryption **“as per appropriate encryption standards”** but does **not specify a particular encryption algorithm/standard** (e.g., AES level, TLS version) in these sections. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.31]
- The retrieved text does not specify concrete technical parameters for face-match accuracy (e.g., FAR/FRR thresholds), only requiring **“high degree of accuracy.”** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, pp.31-32]

retrieved_sections.title: C. Definitions (p.4)
retrieved_sections.text:
Chapter I – Preliminary
A. Short Title and Commencement.
1. These Directions shall be called the Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks –
Know Your Customer) Directions, 2025.
2. These directions shall come into effect on the day they are placed on the official
website of the RBI.
B. Applicability
3. These directions shall be applicable to Commercial Banks (hereinafter collectively
referred to as 'banks' and individually as a 'bank').
For the purpose of these Directions, ‘Commercial Banks’ mean banking companies
(other than Small Finance Banks, Payment Banks, and Local Area Banks),
corresponding new banks, and the State Bank of India, as defined respectively under
clauses (c), (da), and (nc) of Section 5 of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949.
4. These directions shall also apply to those branches and majority-owned subsidiaries
of the bank which are located abroad, to the extent they are not contradictory to the
local laws in the host country, provided that:
(1) where applicable laws and regulations prohibit implementation of these guidelines,
the bank shall bring the same to the notice of the RBI. The RBI may advise the bank
to take further necessary action, including application of additional measures to
manage the ML / TF risks.
(2) in case there is a variance in KYC / AML standards prescribed by the RBI and the
host country regulators, branches / subsidiaries of the bank shall adopt the more
stringent regulation of the two.
(3) branches/ subsidiaries of a Foreign Incorporated bank may adopt the more
stringent regulation of the two i.e., standards prescribed by RBI and their home country
regulators.
Provided that this rule shall not apply to ‘small accounts’ referred to in paragraph 28 of
Chapter VI.
C. Definitions
5. In these Directions, unless the context otherwise requires, the following meanings

OTP based e-KYC authentication as per paragraph 25.
(3) Updation / Periodic updation of KYC for eligible customers.
27. The bank opting to undertake V-CIP, shall adhere to the following minimum
standards:
(1) V-CIP Infrastructure
(i) The bank shall have complied with the RBI guidelines on minimum baseline cyber
security and resilience framework for banks, as updated from time to time as well as
other general guidelines on IT risks. The bank shall house the technology infrastructure
in its own premises and the V-CIP connection and interaction shall necessarily
originate from its own secured network domain. Any technology related outsourcing for
the process shall comply with relevant RBI guidelines. Where the bank uses a cloud
deployment model, it shall ensure that ownership of data in such model rests with the
bank only and all the data including video recording is transferred to the bank’s
exclusively owned / leased server(s) including cloud server, if any, immediately after
the V-CIP process is completed and the cloud service provider or third-party
technology provider assisting the V-CIP shall retain no data.
(ii) The bank shall ensure end-to-end encryption of data between customer device and
the hosting point of the V-CIP application, as per appropriate encryption standards.
The bank shall record the customer consent in an auditable and alteration-proof
manner.
(iii) The V-CIP infrastructure / application shall be capable of preventing connection
from IP addresses outside India or from spoofed IP addresses.
(iv) The video recordings shall contain the live GPS co-ordinates (geo-tagging) of the
customer undertaking the V-CIP and date and time stamp. The quality of the live video
in the V-CIP shall be adequate to allow identification of the customer beyond doubt.
(v) The application shall have components with face liveness / spoof detection as well
as face matching technology with high degree of accuracy, even though the ultimate
responsibility of any customer identification rests with the bank.
Explanation: Making specific facial gestures, like blinking of eyes, smiling, frowning,
etc. is not mandatory for liveness check. The bank shall take due cognizance of special

retrieved_sections.text:
Rupees One Lakh. In case the balance exceeds the threshold, the bank shall cease
the account’s operation, until it completes the CDD as mentioned at (6) below.
(4) The aggregate of all credits in a financial year, in all the deposit accounts taken
together, shall not exceed Rupees Two Lakh.
(5) As regards borrowal accounts, the bank shall sanction only term loans. The
aggregate amount of term loans sanctioned shall not exceed ₹60,000 in a year.
(6) The bank shall not allow accounts, both deposit and borrowal, opened using OTP
based e-KYC to operate for more than one year unless it carries out identification as
per paragraph 23 or as per paragraphs 26 and 27 (V-CIP). If the bank uses Aadhaar
details under paragraphs 26 and 27, it shall follow the process in its entirety, including
fresh Aadhaar OTP authentication.
(7) If the bank does not complete the CDD procedure as mentioned above within a
year; (a) in respect of deposit accounts, the bank shall close the same immediately,
and (b) in respect of borrowal accounts, the bank shall allow no further debits.
(8) The bank shall obtain declaration from the customer to the effect that no other
account has been opened nor will be opened using OTP based KYC in non-face-toface mode with any other RE. Further, while uploading KYC information to CKYCR,
bank shall clearly indicate that such accounts are opened using OTP based e-KYC
and other REs shall not open accounts based on the KYC information of accounts
opened with OTP based e-KYC procedure in non-face- to-face mode.
(9) The bank shall have strict monitoring procedures including systems to generate
alerts in case of any non-compliance / violation, to ensure compliance with the abovementioned conditions.
26. The bank may undertake V-CIP to carry out:
(1) CDD in case of new customer onboarding for individual customers, proprietor in
case of proprietorship firm, authorised signatories and Beneficial Owners (BOs) in case
of Legal Entity (LE) customers.
Provided that in case of CDD of a proprietorship firm, the bank shall also obtain the
equivalent e-document of the activity proofs with respect to the proprietorship firm, as
mentioned in paragraph 31 and paragraph 32, apart from undertaking CDD of the
proprietor.

(vi) The bank may use appropriate artificial intelligence (AI) technology to ensure that

(vii) Based on experience of detected / attempted / ‘near-miss’ cases of forged identity,
the bank shall regularly update the technology infrastructure including application
software as well as workflows. The bank shall report any detected case of forged
identity through V-CIP as a cyber event under extant regulatory guidelines.
(viii) The bank shall subject the V-CIP infrastructure to necessary tests such as
Vulnerability Assessment, Penetration testing and a Security Audit to ensure its
robustness and end-to-end encryption capabilities. The bank shall mitigate any critical
gap reported under this process before rolling out its implementation. The empanelled
auditors of Indian Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT-In) shall conduct
such tests. Such tests shall also be carried out periodically in conformance to internal
/ regulatory guidelines.
(ix) The bank shall subject the V-CIP application software and relevant APIs /
webservices to appropriate testing of functional, performance, and maintenance
strength before being used in live environment. The bank shall roll out the application
only after closure of any critical gap found during such tests. Such tests shall also be
carried out periodically in conformity with internal / regulatory guidelines.
(2) V-CIP Procedure
(i) Each bank shall formulate a clear workflow and standard operating procedure for VCIP and ensure adherence to it. The V-CIP process shall be operated only by officials
of the bank specially trained for this purpose. The official shall be capable to carry out
liveness check and detect any other fraudulent manipulation or suspicious conduct of
the customer and act upon it. The liveness check shall not result in exclusion of person
with special needs.

The bank shall ensure that it also confirms the economic and financial profile /
information submitted by the customer from the customer undertaking the V-CIP in a
suitable manner.
(xi) The bank shall capture a clear image of PAN card displayed by the customer during
the process, except in cases where e-PAN is provided by the customer. The bank shall
verify the PAN details from the database of the issuing authority ,including through
DigiLocker.
(xii) The use of printed copy of equivalent e-document, including an e-PAN is not valid
for the V-CIP.
(xiii) The authorised official of the bank shall ensure that photograph of the customer
in the Aadhaar / OVD and PAN / e-PAN matches with the customer undertaking the VCIP and the identification details in Aadhaar / OVD and PAN / e-PAN shall match with
the details provided by the customer.
(xiv) The bank shall permit assisted V-CIP when it takes help of Business
Correspondents (BCs) to facilitate the process only at the customer end. The bank
shall maintain the details of the BC assisting the customer, where it utilises the services
of BCs. The bank shall have the ultimate responsibility for customer due diligence.
(xv) The bank shall make all accounts opened through V-CIP operational only after
subjecting them to concurrent audit to ensure the integrity of process and its
acceptability of its outcome.
(xvi) The bank shall appropriately comply with all matters not specified under the
paragraph but required under other statutes such as the Information Technology (IT)
Act.
(3) V-CIP Records and Data Management
(i) The bank shall store the entire data and recordings of V-CIP in a system / systems
located in India. The bank shall ensure that the video recording is stored in a safe and
secure manner and bears the date and time stamp that affords easy historical data
search. The extant instructions on record management, as stipulated in this direction,
shall also apply to V-CIP.
(ii) The bank shall preserve the activity log along with the credentials of the official
performing the V-CIP.

retrieved_sections.text:
indicating the liveness of the interaction, during video interactions to establish that the
interactions are real-time and not pre-recorded.
(iv) The bank shall reject the account opening process if it observes any prompting at
the customer end.
(v) The bank shall factor in the fact that the V-CIP customer is an existing or new
customer, or if the case relates to one rejected earlier or if the name appears in some
negative list, at an appropriate stage of workflow.
(vi) The authorised official of the bank performing the V-CIP shall record audio and
video as well as capture a photograph of the customer present for identification and
obtain the identification information using any one of the following:
(a) OTP based Aadhaar e-KYC authentication.
(b) Offline Verification of Aadhaar for identification.
(c) KYC records downloaded from CKYCR, in accordance with paragraph 65, using
the KYC identifier provided by the customer.
(d) Equivalent e-document of Officially Valid Documents (OVDs) including documents
issued through DigiLocker.
(vii) The bank shall ensure to redact or blackout the Aadhaar number in terms of
paragraph 23.
(viii) In case of offline verification of Aadhaar using XML file or Aadhaar Secure QR
Code, the bank shall ensure that the XML file or QR code generation date is not older
than three working days from the date of carrying out V-CIP.
(ix) Further, in line with the prescribed period of three working days for usage of
Aadhaar XML file / Aadhaar QR code, the bank shall ensure that it undertakes video
process of the V-CIP within three working days of downloading / obtaining the
identification information through CKYCR / Aadhaar authentication / equivalent edocument, if in the rare cases, the entire process cannot be completed at one go or
seamlessly. However, the bank shall ensure that no incremental risk is added due to
this.
(x) If the address of the customer is different from that indicated in the OVD, the bank
shall capture suitable records of the current address, as per the existing requirement.

inferred_points.point: V-CIP cannot be completed where the customer’s connection originates from outside India, because the V-CIP infrastructure/application must prevent connections from IP addresses outside India.
supporting_definitions: “The V-CIP infrastructure / application shall be capable of preventing connection from IP addresses outside India or from spoofed IP addresses.”
inferred_points.reasoning: If the application must prevent connections from IP addresses outside India, then a customer attempting V-CIP from a non-India IP address would be blocked, implying V-CIP completion is not feasible from outside-India IP origination (subject to spoofing controls).

inferred_points.point: Banks must ensure a post-process data handover and non-retention control framework for cloud/third-party providers assisting V-CIP, because data must be transferred immediately to bank-owned/leased servers and third parties must retain no data.
supporting_definitions: “Where the bank uses a cloud deployment model, it shall ensure that ownership of data in such model rests with the bank only and all the data including video recording is transferred to the bank’s exclusively owned / leased server(s) including cloud server, if any, immediately after the V-CIP process is completed and the cloud service provider or third-party technology provider assisting the V-CIP shall retain no data.”
inferred_points.reasoning: Immediate transfer to bank-owned/leased servers combined with a requirement that third parties retain no data logically entails that banks must operationalize controls/processes to effect immediate transfer and ensure third-party non-retention (e.g., technical/contractual and monitoring controls), even though specific mechanisms are not detailed in the text.

inferred_points.point: V-CIP performance by bank officials is constrained to bank-controlled network environments, because the interaction must originate from the bank’s secured network domain and the infrastructure must be housed in the bank’s own premises.
supporting_definitions: “The bank shall house the technology infrastructure in its own premises and the V-CIP connection and interaction shall necessarily originate from its own secured network domain.”
inferred_points.reasoning: If the V-CIP connection and interaction must originate from the bank’s secured network domain, then conducting V-CIP from non-bank network domains would not meet this condition, implying operational constraints requiring bank-controlled network origination for the V-CIP session.

query_text: Which limits are breached (if any), what immediate operational restrictions must be applied, and by what deadline must the bank complete full identification (and what happens if it doesn’t)? Include how you’d handle the required customer declaration about not having OTP-based accounts elsewhere.
sub_queries: Which monetary/transactional limits are breached when a customer holds OTP-based/limited-KYC accounts beyond permitted thresholds?
What immediate operational restrictions must the bank impose when those limits are breached?
By what deadline must the bank complete the customer's full identification (KYC) after breach/trigger?
What are the consequences/steps if the bank fails to complete full identification by the deadline?
What is the required customer declaration about not having OTP-based accounts elsewhere and how should the bank handle/record it?
answer_text:
## 1) Limits to test for breach (and whether any are breached)
The retrieved sections do not provide your customer’s *actual* balances/credits/loan sanctions, so it is not possible to conclude whether a breach has occurred without those figures. What can be stated precisely is the regulatory limit-set you must test against for **accounts opened using Aadhaar OTP based e-KYC in non-face-to-face mode**:

### Deposit-side limits (OTP-based e-KYC, non-face-to-face)
- **Aggregate balance cap (all deposit accounts, taken together):** “**The aggregate balance of all the deposit accounts of the customer shall not exceed Rupees One Lakh.**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.29), p.29]
- **Aggregate annual credits cap (all deposit accounts, taken together):** “**The aggregate of all credits in a financial year, in all the deposit accounts taken together, shall not exceed Rupees Two Lakh.**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

### Borrowal-side limits (OTP-based e-KYC, non-face-to-face)
- **Only term loans permitted:** “**As regards borrowal accounts, the bank shall sanction only term loans.**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]
- **Annual cap on term loans sanctioned:** “**The aggregate amount of term loans sanctioned shall not exceed ₹60,000 in a year.**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

## 2) Immediate operational restrictions to apply
### A. If the aggregate balance exceeds ₹1,00,000
- The Direction requires an immediate stoppage of operations: “**In case the balance exceeds the threshold, the bank shall cease the account’s operation, until it completes the CDD** …” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]
  - This is an immediate operational control: cease operations **until** completion of the specified CDD/identification route. [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

### B. Controls that must be in place for OTP-based e-KYC accounts (ongoing operational restrictions/controls)
- **Customer consent for OTP authentication:** “**The Customer shall give specific consent for the authentication through OTP.**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.29), p.29]
- **Send transaction alerts/OTPs only to Aadhaar-registered mobile; have policy for mobile change:** as a “**risk-mitigating measure**,” the bank must ensure it “**sends transaction alerts, OTP, etc., only to the mobile number of the customer registered with Aadhaar**” and must have “**a Board-approved policy delineating a robust process of due diligence for dealing with requests for change of mobile number in such accounts.**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.29), p.29]
- **Monitoring and alerts for non-compliance/violation:** the bank must have “**strict monitoring procedures including systems to generate alerts in case of any non-compliance / violation**” to ensure compliance with the conditions. [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

## 3) Deadline to complete full identification (CDD) and consequences if not completed
### Deadline
- The account cannot continue to operate beyond one year on the basis of OTP-based e-KYC alone: “**The bank shall not allow accounts, both deposit and borrowal, opened using OTP based e-KYC to operate for more than one year unless it carries out identification as per paragraph 23 or as per paragraphs 26 and 27 (V-CIP).**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]
- If the bank uses Aadhaar details under V-CIP for this purpose, it must follow the full process “**including fresh Aadhaar OTP authentication.**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

### What happens if it doesn’t complete within one year
If the bank does not complete the required CDD/identification “**within a year**”:
- **Deposit accounts:** “**the bank shall close the same immediately**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]
- **Borrowal accounts:** “**the bank shall allow no further debits**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

## 4) Handling the required customer declaration about not having OTP-based accounts elsewhere
For accounts opened using OTP based e-KYC in non-face-to-face mode:
- The bank “**shall obtain declaration from the customer to the effect that no other account has been opened nor will be opened using OTP based KYC in non-face-to-face mode with any other RE.**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]
- Additionally, when uploading KYC information to CKYCR, the bank must “**clearly indicate that such accounts are opened using OTP based e-KYC**” and that “**other REs shall not open accounts based on the KYC information of accounts opened with OTP based e-KYC procedure in non-face- to-face mode.**” [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

### Practical operationalization (limited to what the text supports)
- Operationally, you must (i) collect the customer’s declaration with the specified wording/meaning, and (ii) ensure CKYCR upload is flagged exactly as required (i.e., clearly indicating OTP-based e-KYC, non-face-to-face), because both are stated as mandatory “shall” requirements. [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

## Information missing to answer “which limits are breached” definitively
To determine breaches, you would need at least:
- Current **aggregate balance** across all deposit accounts opened using OTP-based e-KYC (to compare to ₹1,00,000 cap). [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.29), p.29]
- **Aggregate credits** in the current financial year across all deposit accounts (to compare to ₹2,00,000 cap). [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]
- For borrowal: confirmation that only **term loans** were sanctioned and the **aggregate sanctioned** in the year (to compare to ₹60,000 cap). [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]
- Account opening date (or date of OTP e-KYC onboarding) to measure the **one-year** CDD completion deadline. [CDD Procedure in case of Individuals (p.30), p.30]

retrieved_sections.title: C. Definitions (p.4)
retrieved_sections.text:
Chapter I – Preliminary
A. Short Title and Commencement.
1. These Directions shall be called the Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks –
Know Your Customer) Directions, 2025.
2. These directions shall come into effect on the day they are placed on the official
website of the RBI.
B. Applicability
3. These directions shall be applicable to Commercial Banks (hereinafter collectively
referred to as 'banks' and individually as a 'bank').
For the purpose of these Directions, ‘Commercial Banks’ mean banking companies
(other than Small Finance Banks, Payment Banks, and Local Area Banks),
corresponding new banks, and the State Bank of India, as defined respectively under
clauses (c), (da), and (nc) of Section 5 of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949.
4. These directions shall also apply to those branches and majority-owned subsidiaries
of the bank which are located abroad, to the extent they are not contradictory to the
local laws in the host country, provided that:
(1) where applicable laws and regulations prohibit implementation of these guidelines,
the bank shall bring the same to the notice of the RBI. The RBI may advise the bank
to take further necessary action, including application of additional measures to
manage the ML / TF risks.
(2) in case there is a variance in KYC / AML standards prescribed by the RBI and the
host country regulators, branches / subsidiaries of the bank shall adopt the more
stringent regulation of the two.
(3) branches/ subsidiaries of a Foreign Incorporated bank may adopt the more
stringent regulation of the two i.e., standards prescribed by RBI and their home country
regulators.
Provided that this rule shall not apply to ‘small accounts’ referred to in paragraph 28 of
Chapter VI.
C. Definitions
5. In these Directions, unless the context otherwise requires, the following meanings

retrieved_sections.title: C. Definitions (p.6)
retrieved_sections.text:
the beneficial owner is the natural person(s), who, whether acting alone or together, or
through one or more juridical person, has / have ownership of / entitlement to more than
15 percent of the property or capital or profits of the unincorporated association or body
of individuals.
Explanation: Term ‘body of individuals’ includes societies. Where no natural person is
identified under (a), (b) or (c) above, the beneficial owner is the relevant natural person
who holds the position of senior managing official.
(d) Where the customer is a trust, the identification of beneficial owner(s) shall include
identification of the author of the trust, the trustee, the beneficiaries with 10 percent or
more interest in the trust and any other natural person exercising ultimate effective
control over the trust through a chain of control or ownership.
(v) ‘Certified Copy’ – Obtaining the certified copy by the bank shall mean comparing
the copy of the proof of possession of Aadhaar number where offline verification cannot
be carried out or the officially valid document produced by the customer with the
original, and an authorised officer of the bank shall record the comparison on the copy
as per the provisions contained in the Act. Provided that in case of Non-Resident
Indians (NRIs) and Persons of Indian Origin (PIOs), as defined in Foreign Exchange
Management (Deposit) Regulations, 2016 {FEMA 5(R)}, the bank may alternatively
obtain the original certified copy, certified by any one of the following:
(a) authorised officials of overseas branches of Scheduled Commercial Banks
registered in India,
(b) branches of overseas banks with whom Indian banks have relationships,
(c) Notary Public abroad,
(d) Court Magistrate,
(e) Judge,
(f) Indian Embassy / Consulate General in the country where the non-resident
customer resides.
(vi) ‘Central KYC Records Registry’ (CKYCR) means an entity defined under Rule 2(1)
of the Rules, to receive, store, safeguard and retrieve the KYC records in digital form
of a customer.

Rupees One Lakh. In case the balance exceeds the threshold, the bank shall cease
the account’s operation, until it completes the CDD as mentioned at (6) below.
(4) The aggregate of all credits in a financial year, in all the deposit accounts taken
together, shall not exceed Rupees Two Lakh.
(5) As regards borrowal accounts, the bank shall sanction only term loans. The
aggregate amount of term loans sanctioned shall not exceed ₹60,000 in a year.
(6) The bank shall not allow accounts, both deposit and borrowal, opened using OTP

per paragraph 23 or as per paragraphs 26 and 27 (V-CIP). If the bank uses Aadhaar

fresh Aadhaar OTP authentication.
(7) If the bank does not complete the CDD procedure as mentioned above within a
year; (a) in respect of deposit accounts, the bank shall close the same immediately,
and (b) in respect of borrowal accounts, the bank shall allow no further debits.
(8) The bank shall obtain declaration from the customer to the effect that no other
account has been opened nor will be opened using OTP based KYC in non-face-toface mode with any other RE. Further, while uploading KYC information to CKYCR,
bank shall clearly indicate that such accounts are opened using OTP based e-KYC
and other REs shall not open accounts based on the KYC information of accounts
opened with OTP based e-KYC procedure in non-face- to-face mode.
(9) The bank shall have strict monitoring procedures including systems to generate
alerts in case of any non-compliance / violation, to ensure compliance with the abovementioned conditions.
26. The bank may undertake V-CIP to carry out:
(1) CDD in case of new customer onboarding for individual customers, proprietor in
case of proprietorship firm, authorised signatories and Beneficial Owners (BOs) in case
of Legal Entity (LE) customers.
Provided that in case of CDD of a proprietorship firm, the bank shall also obtain the
equivalent e-document of the activity proofs with respect to the proprietorship firm, as
mentioned in paragraph 31 and paragraph 32, apart from undertaking CDD of the
proprietor.

inferred_points.point: If the bank completes identification (CDD) as per paragraph 23 or via paragraphs 26 and 27 (V-CIP) within one year, the OTP-based e-KYC account is not subject to the ‘not allow ... to operate for more than one year’ stoppage on that ground.
supporting_definitions: “The bank shall not allow accounts, both deposit and borrowal, opened using OTP based e-KYC to operate for more than one year unless it carries out identification as per paragraph 23 or as per paragraphs 26 and 27 (V-CIP).”
inferred_points.reasoning: The rule prohibits operation beyond one year unless identification is carried out via specified routes. Logically, completion of identification via those routes within the one-year period satisfies the ‘unless’ condition, removing that specific one-year operational restriction.

inferred_points.point: If the customer’s aggregate balance across all deposit accounts does not exceed ₹1,00,000, the specific mandatory action to ‘cease the account’s operation’ triggered by exceeding the balance threshold is not triggered.
supporting_definitions: “In case the balance exceeds the threshold, the bank shall cease the account’s operation, until it completes the CDD...”
“The aggregate balance of all the deposit accounts of the customer shall not exceed Rupees One Lakh.”
inferred_points.reasoning: The cessation requirement is expressly conditional on the balance exceeding the threshold. If the balance does not exceed that threshold, the condition precedent is not met; therefore, that particular cessation action is not required on that ground.

inferred_points.point: Failure to complete CDD within one year results in an immediate, non-discretionary outcome for both product types: deposit accounts must be closed immediately and borrowal accounts must be frozen for further debits, irrespective of whether other numeric caps were complied with.
supporting_definitions: “If the bank does not complete the CDD procedure as mentioned above within a year; (a) in respect of deposit accounts, the bank shall close the same immediately, and (b) in respect of borrowal accounts, the bank shall allow no further debits.”
inferred_points.reasoning: The consequence clause is written as a standalone condition tied only to non-completion of CDD within one year, and uses mandatory ‘shall’ language. It does not state that compliance with balance/credit/loan caps provides an exception; therefore the consequence applies regardless of other compliance.

inferred_points.point: Because the bank ‘shall obtain declaration’ that no other OTP-based non-face-to-face account has been/will be opened with any other RE, an operational control is required to capture and retain this declaration as part of the onboarding/maintenance process for such accounts.
supporting_definitions: “The bank shall obtain declaration from the customer to the effect that no other account has been opened nor will be opened using OTP based KYC in non-face-toface mode with any other RE.”
inferred_points.reasoning: A ‘shall obtain declaration’ requirement necessarily entails implementing a process step and record capture to actually obtain (and therefore evidence) the declaration for the relevant account type. The text does not specify exact timing, but operationally it must be obtained in a manner that ensures compliance for those accounts.

query_text: Identify all Beneficial Owners you must determine for X, and explain why each qualifies (ownership vs control vs “senior managing official” fallback). Also list whose CDD you must perform (customer, BOs, authorised signatories, POA holders, etc.) and what evidence you’d rely on.
sub_queries: Which persons/entities qualify as Beneficial Owners by ownership (percentage thresholds and aggregation rules)?
Which persons/entities qualify as Beneficial Owners by control (control through other means, e.g., voting rights, board control, nominee arrangements)?
When and how the 'senior managing official' fallback applies and who that is?

Which categories of people/entities require CDD in relation to a customer (customer, BOs, authorised signatories, POA holders, authorised representatives, controllers, natural persons behind legal persons)?

What types of documentary or non-documentary evidence are relied on for identity and for proving ownership/control for each category (e.g., passport, PAN, incorporation docs, share registers, trust deeds, board resolutions, POA)?
answer_text:
{
  "answer_text": "## 1) Beneficial Owners (BOs) you must determine for “X” — depends on the legal form of X

Because the BO definition is legal-form specific, the BO set you must identify for X depends on whether X is a **company**, **partnership firm**, **unincorporated association/body of individuals**, or a **trust**. The RBI definition is always in terms of **natural person(s)** (even where ownership/control is exercised through other entities). [C. Definitions, p.5]

### A. If X is a **company**
You must determine as BO(s) the **natural person(s)** who (acting alone / together / through one or more juridical persons):
- **Ownership basis (threshold test):** have a “**controlling ownership interest**”, i.e., “**ownership of / entitlement to more than 10 percent of the shares or capital or profits of the company**”. This qualifies them as BOs on an **ownership** basis. [C. Definitions, p.5]
- **Control basis (control through other means):** even if they do not cross the ownership threshold, any natural person who “**exercises control through other means**” qualifies as a BO on a **control** basis. “**Control**” is defined to “**include the right to appoint the majority of the directors or to control the management or policy decisions including by virtue of their shareholding or management rights or shareholders agreements or voting agreements**.” [C. Definitions, p.5]

### B. If X is a **partnership firm**
You must determine as BO(s) the natural person(s) who (acting alone / together / through one or more juridical persons):
- **Ownership basis (threshold test):** have “**ownership of / entitlement to more than 10 percent of capital or profits of the partnership**.” [C. Definitions, p.5]
- **Control basis:** or who “**exercises control through other means**,” where “control” “**shall include the right to control the management or policy decision**.” [C. Definitions, p.5]

### C. If X is an **unincorporated association or body of individuals** (including societies)
You must determine as BO(s) the natural person(s) who (acting alone / together / through one or more juridical persons):
- **Ownership basis (threshold test):** have “**ownership of / entitlement to more than 15 percent of the property or capital or profits**” of the unincorporated association/body of individuals. [C. Definitions, p.6]
- **Senior managing official fallback (only if no BO identified under the threshold/control tests for (a)/(b)/(c)):** “**Where no natural person is identified under (a), (b) or (c) above, the beneficial owner is the relevant natural person who holds the position of senior managing official.**” This qualifies as the BO on a **fallback** basis. [C. Definitions, p.6]

Notes relevant to classification:
- “**body of individuals includes societies**.” [C. Definitions, p.6]
- For documentation purposes, “**Unregistered trusts / partnership firms shall be included under the term ‘unincorporated association’.**” [D. Identification of Beneficial Owner, p.39]

### D. If X is a **trust**
The BO identification for a trust is not a single threshold owner test. Instead, “**the identification of beneficial owner(s) shall include identification of**” all of the following categories (each qualifies because the regulation explicitly includes them in the BO set):
- **Author of the trust** [C. Definitions, p.6]
- **Trustee** [C. Definitions, p.6]
- **Beneficiaries with 10 percent or more interest in the trust** (threshold inclusion) [C. Definitions, p.6]
- **Any other natural person exercising ultimate effective control over the trust through a chain of control or ownership** (control inclusion) [C. Definitions, p.6]

## 2) BO identification & verification obligation (what you must do once you determine who the BOs are)

### A. When opening an account for a legal person
“For opening an account of a **Legal Person** who is not a natural person, the bank shall **identify the beneficial owner(s)** and shall undertake **all reasonable steps** … **to verify their identity**….” [D. Identification of Beneficial Owner, p.39]

### B. Exception for listed entities (and certain related entities)
Where “**the customer or the owner of the controlling interest**” is:
- “**an entity listed on a stock exchange in India**”, or
- “**an entity resident in jurisdictions notified by the Central Government and listed on stock exchanges in such jurisdictions**”, or
- “**a subsidiary of such listed entities**”,
then “**it is not necessary to identify and verify the identity of any shareholder or beneficial owner of such entities**.” [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44]

## 3) Whose CDD you must perform (customer, BOs, authorised signatories, POA holders, etc.)

### A. Individuals covered by the CDD procedure (explicit scope)
For CDD, the bank must obtain prescribed KYC inputs from an individual while:
- “**establishing an account-based relationship**” with the individual (i.e., where the customer is an individual), OR
- “**dealing with the individual who is a beneficial owner, authorised signatory or the power of attorney holder related to any legal entity**.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.25]

Accordingly, within the scope of the provided text, CDD is explicitly required for:
- the **individual customer** (where the customer is an individual) [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.25]
- each **individual beneficial owner** of a legal entity customer [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.25]
- each **individual authorised signatory** of a legal entity customer [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.25]
- each **individual power of attorney (POA) holder** transacting “related to any legal entity” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.25]

### B. Persons acting on behalf of certain juridical persons (authorised persons and attorney holders)
For opening an account of certain juridical persons (e.g., “**societies, universities and local bodies like village panchayats, etc.**”), or where someone “**purports to act on behalf of such juridical person or individual or trust**,” the bank must obtain and verify certified copies of:
- “**Document showing name of the person authorised to act on behalf of the entity**” [D. Identification of Beneficial Owner, p.39]
- “**Documents, as specified in paragraph 23, of the person holding an attorney to transact on its behalf**” [D. Identification of Beneficial Owner, p.39]
- “**Such documents as may be required by the bank to establish the legal existence of such an entity / juridical person**.” [D. Identification of Beneficial Owner, p.39]

### C. Trust/nominee/fiduciary arrangements — intermediaries and persons represented
“In cases of **trust / nominee or fiduciary accounts**, the bank determines whether the customer is acting on behalf of another person as trustee / nominee or any other intermediary.” In such cases, the bank shall obtain “**satisfactory evidence of the identity of the intermediaries and of the persons on whose behalf they are acting, as well as details of the nature of the trust or other arrangements in place**.” [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44]

## 4) Evidence you would rely on (CDD/KYC evidence for each relevant person)

### A. Evidence for any individual for whom CDD is required (customer / BO / authorised signatory / POA holder)
The CDD inputs the bank must obtain from the individual include:
- **Aadhaar number** in specified cases, including when they “**decide to submit their Aadhaar number voluntarily to a bank** …” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.25]
- **Proof of possession of Aadhaar number** where the bank can carry out offline verification [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.25]
- **Proof of possession of Aadhaar number (where offline verification cannot be carried out) or any OVD** (or “**equivalent e-document**”) containing identity and address details [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.25]
- **KYC Identifier** with explicit consent to download from CKYCR [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.25]
- **PAN** (or equivalent e-document) or **Form No. 60** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.25]
- Any other documents the bank may require, including regarding “**nature of business and financial status**” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.25]

### B. Verification / reliance pathway tied to what the individual submits
Depending on what is provided, the bank’s reliance/verification mechanism is explicitly specified:
- If Aadhaar number is submitted under the Aadhaar-number clause, the bank “**shall carry out authentication … using UIDAI’s e-KYC authentication facility**.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.25]
- If proof of possession of Aadhaar is submitted where offline verification can be carried out, the bank “**shall carry out offline verification**.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.26]
- If an “**equivalent e-document of any OVD**” is submitted, the bank shall “**verify the digital signature**” as per IT Act and “**take a live photo**” as specified under paragraph 24. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.26]
- If any OVD / proof of possession of Aadhaar is submitted where offline verification cannot be carried out, the bank shall carry out “**verification through digital KYC**” as specified under paragraph 24. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.26]
- If KYC Identifier is submitted, the bank shall “**retrieve the KYC records online from the CKYCR** ….” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.26]

### C. Evidence for the legal entity/juridical person itself (legal existence + authority)
For relevant juridical persons, the bank relies on:
- document evidencing who is “**authorised to act on behalf of the entity**” [D. Identification of Beneficial Owner, p.39]
- the paragraph 23 CDD document set for the “**person holding an attorney to transact on its behalf**” [D. Identification of Beneficial Owner, p.39; A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.25]
- documents sufficient to “**establish the legal existence of such an entity / juridical person**.” [D. Identification of Beneficial Owner, p.39]

### D. Evidence in trust/nominee/fiduciary arrangements
Where a customer is acting via trustee/nominee/intermediary, reliance includes “**satisfactory evidence**” of:
- identity of “**intermediaries**”
- identity of “**persons on whose behalf they are acting**”
- and “**details of the nature of the trust or other arrangements in place**.” [E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44]

## 5) What is missing to answer “for X” as a concrete case
The provided sections do not describe what “X” is (company/partnership/trust/etc.), whether X (or controlling-interest owner) is listed (which can remove the BO identification/verification requirement), or the ownership/control structure (shareholding %, partner profit share %, trust deed/beneficiary interests, control rights). Without those facts, BO identification can only be stated in the above rule-based form. [C. Definitions, pp.5-6; E. On-going Due Diligence, pp.40-44]

retrieved_sections.title: C. Definitions (p.6)
retrieved_sections.text:
the beneficial owner is the natural person(s), who, whether acting alone or together, or
through one or more juridical person, has / have ownership of / entitlement to more than
15 percent of the property or capital or profits of the unincorporated association or body
of individuals.
Explanation: Term ‘body of individuals’ includes societies. Where no natural person is
identified under (a), (b) or (c) above, the beneficial owner is the relevant natural person
who holds the position of senior managing official.
(d) Where the customer is a trust, the identification of beneficial owner(s) shall include
identification of the author of the trust, the trustee, the beneficiaries with 10 percent or
more interest in the trust and any other natural person exercising ultimate effective
control over the trust through a chain of control or ownership.
(v) ‘Certified Copy’ – Obtaining the certified copy by the bank shall mean comparing
the copy of the proof of possession of Aadhaar number where offline verification cannot
be carried out or the officially valid document produced by the customer with the
original, and an authorised officer of the bank shall record the comparison on the copy
as per the provisions contained in the Act. Provided that in case of Non-Resident
Indians (NRIs) and Persons of Indian Origin (PIOs), as defined in Foreign Exchange
Management (Deposit) Regulations, 2016 {FEMA 5(R)}, the bank may alternatively
obtain the original certified copy, certified by any one of the following:
(a) authorised officials of overseas branches of Scheduled Commercial Banks
registered in India,
(b) branches of overseas banks with whom Indian banks have relationships,
(c) Notary Public abroad,
(d) Court Magistrate,
(e) Judge,
(f) Indian Embassy / Consulate General in the country where the non-resident
customer resides.
(vi) ‘Central KYC Records Registry’ (CKYCR) means an entity defined under Rule 2(1)
of the Rules, to receive, store, safeguard and retrieve the KYC records in digital form
of a customer.

inferred_points.point: For any individual who is a BO, authorised signatory, or POA holder related to a legal entity, the bank should apply the same individual CDD input set (Aadhaar/OVD/CKYCR route + PAN/Form 60, etc.) as it would for an individual customer, because paragraph 23 applies identically to those roles.
supporting_definitions: “For undertaking CDD, the bank shall obtain the following from an individual while establishing an account-based relationship or while dealing with the individual who is a beneficial owner, authorised signatory or the power of attorney holder related to any legal entity:”
inferred_points.reasoning: The regulation uses a single CDD rule for an “individual” that explicitly covers (i) an individual customer being onboarded and (ii) any individual BO/authorised signatory/POA holder related to a legal entity. Therefore, the document/data inputs listed in paragraph 23 logically apply to each of those roles.

inferred_points.point: If X qualifies for the listed-entity (or subsidiary) exemption, BO identification/verification should not be performed for X’s shareholders/BOs (and consequently the bank would not need to complete BO-level identity verification steps), because the Directions state it is “not necessary” to identify and verify BOs in those cases.
supporting_definitions: “it is not necessary to identify and verify the identity of any shareholder or beneficial owner of such entities.”
“For opening an account of a Legal Person who is not a natural person, the bank shall identify the beneficial owner(s)… [and] verify their identity…”
inferred_points.reasoning: The general rule requires BO identification and verification for legal-person customers, but the listed-entity provision states that in specified listed/subsidiary cases it is not necessary to identify and verify any shareholder or BO. This creates a clear carve-out from the general BO obligation for those entities.

inferred_points.point: For trusts, the Directions’ explicit inclusion of only “beneficiaries with 10 percent or more interest” implies that beneficiaries below 10% interest are not included as BOs solely by virtue of being beneficiaries (though they could still fall within other included categories, e.g., exercising ultimate effective control).
supporting_definitions: “the identification of beneficial owner(s) shall include identification of… the beneficiaries with 10 percent or more interest in the trust and any other natural person exercising ultimate effective control over the trust…”
inferred_points.reasoning: The trust BO rule applies a specific quantitative threshold to beneficiaries (“10 percent or more”). This logically means beneficiary status alone does not trigger BO inclusion below that threshold, unless the person is captured by another BO limb (e.g., ultimate effective control).

inferred_points.point: The “senior managing official” BO fallback applies not only to unincorporated associations/body of individuals, but also to company and partnership customers, because the fallback is triggered when no natural person is identified under (a), (b) or (c) (i.e., company, partnership firm, and unincorporated association/body of individuals).
supporting_definitions: “Where the customer is a company, the beneficial owner is the natural person(s)… has / have a controlling ownership interest or who exercises control through other means.”

“Where the customer is a partnership firm, the beneficial owner is the natural person(s)… has / have ownership of / entitlement to more than 10 percent of capital or profits of the partnership or who exercises control through other means.”

“[W]here the customer is an unincorporated association or body of individuals, the beneficial owner is the natural person(s)… has / have ownership of / entitlement to more than 15 percent of the property or capital or profits of the unincorporated association or body of individuals.”
“Where no natural person is identified under (a), (b) or (c) above, the beneficial owner is the relevant natural person who holds the position of senior managing official.”
inferred_points.reasoning: The regulation defines BO tests for (a) company, (b) partnership firm, and (c) unincorporated association/body of individuals, and then states that where no natural person is identified under (a), (b) or (c), the BO becomes the relevant natural person holding the position of senior managing official. Because the fallback explicitly references (a), (b) and (c), it logically applies to all three customer types.

inferred_points.point: Where the listed-entity exemption applies, the bank is not expected to perform BO identification/verification steps (and therefore cannot be expected to perform individual CDD for BOs) because the regulation removes the requirement to “identify and verify” any shareholder or beneficial owner for such entities.
supporting_definitions: “Where the customer or the owner of the controlling interest is: (i) an entity listed on a stock exchange in India, or (ii) it is an entity resident in jurisdictions notified by the Central Government and listed on stock exchanges in such jurisdictions, or (iii) it is a subsidiary of such listed entities; it is not necessary to identify and verify the identity of any shareholder or beneficial owner of such entities.”
“For undertaking CDD, the bank shall obtain the following from an individual while… dealing with the individual who is a beneficial owner…”
inferred_points.reasoning: The regulation expressly states that, for listed entities (and specified related entities), “it is not necessary to identify and verify the identity of any shareholder or beneficial owner.” Since the CDD procedure for individuals is triggered when the bank is “dealing with the individual who is a beneficial owner,” the exemption logically entails that this BO-specific CDD pathway is not required where the bank is not required to identify/verify any BO at all for such entities.

inferred_points.point: The “senior managing official” BO test is a fallback that applies only after the bank is unable to identify any natural person BO under the company/partnership/unincorporated association tests; therefore, banks must first attempt BO identification under the applicable ownership/control criteria before treating a senior managing official as BO.
supporting_definitions: “Where no natural person is identified under (a), (b) or (c) above, the beneficial owner is the relevant natural person who holds the position of senior managing official.”
inferred_points.reasoning: The regulation explicitly conditions the senior managing official approach on the situation “Where no natural person is identified under (a), (b) or (c) above”. This logically implies sequencing: the bank must apply (a)/(b)/(c) first, and only if that yields no natural person BO may it use the senior managing official as the BO.

inferred_points.point: For companies and partnership firms, persons holding exactly 10% do not meet the “controlling ownership interest” / ownership threshold for BO identification because the threshold is “more than 10 percent”; however, they could still be BOs if they meet the control test.
supporting_definitions: “‘Controlling ownership interest’ means ownership of / entitlement to more than 10 percent of the shares or capital or profits of the company.”
“Where the customer is a partnership firm… has / have ownership of / entitlement to more than 10 percent of capital or profits of the partnership or who exercises control through other means.”
inferred_points.reasoning: The ownership threshold is explicitly stated as “more than 10 percent,” which excludes exactly 10%. Separately, the definition also includes BOs who “exercise control through other means,” so a person at or below the ownership threshold may still qualify under the control limb.

inferred_points.point: For unincorporated associations / bodies of individuals, persons holding exactly 15% do not meet the BO ownership threshold because the threshold is “more than 15 percent”.
supporting_definitions: “…unincorporated association or body of individuals… has / have ownership of / entitlement to more than 15 percent of the property or capital or profits…”
inferred_points.reasoning: The threshold is stated as “more than 15 percent,” which logically excludes exactly 15%.

inferred_points.point: CDD obligations explicitly extend beyond the customer to individuals who are BOs, authorised signatories, or power of attorney holders related to a legal entity; therefore, banks must collect the paragraph 23 CDD inputs for those individuals even if they are not themselves the ‘customer’.
supporting_definitions: “For undertaking CDD, the bank shall obtain the following from an individual while establishing an account-based relationship or while dealing with the individual who is a beneficial owner, authorised signatory or the power of attorney holder related to any legal entity:”
inferred_points.reasoning: The provision creates two triggering contexts for collecting CDD inputs: (i) establishing an account-based relationship, and (ii) dealing with an individual in the specified non-customer roles (BO/authorised signatory/POA holder). This logically entails that the CDD input set is required for those role-holders even when they are not the accountholding customer.

inferred_points.point: Where the listed-entity exemption applies, the bank is relieved from identifying/verifying shareholders/beneficial owners of that entity; therefore, BO identification/verification steps are not required for those entities even though the general rule requires BO identification for legal persons.
supporting_definitions: “it is not necessary to identify and verify the identity of any shareholder or beneficial owner of such entities.”
“For opening an account of a Legal Person who is not a natural person, the bank shall identify the beneficial owner(s) and shall undertake all reasonable steps … to verify their identity…”
inferred_points.reasoning: The general rule mandates BO identification and verification for legal persons. The listed-entity clause states that it is “not necessary” to identify and verify any shareholder or beneficial owner for the specified listed/subsidiary cases, which logically operates as an exception to the general requirement in those cases.

query_text: Which limits are breached (if any), what immediate operational restrictions must be applied, and by what deadline must the bank complete full identification (and what happens if it doesn’t)? Include how you’d handle the required customer declaration about not having OTP-based accounts elsewhere.
sub_queries: What specific transaction/holding limits trigger restrictions or actions when identification is incomplete?
What immediate operational restrictions must the bank impose when those limits are breached?
By what deadline must the bank complete full identification once the trigger occurs?
What are the consequences if the bank does not complete identification by the deadline?
What are the requirements and handling procedure for the customer declaration that they do not have OTP-based accounts elsewhere?
answer_text:
## Applicability (regulatory context)
The limits and restrictions below apply specifically to **“Accounts opened using Aadhaar OTP based e-KYC, in non-face-to-face mode”** under paragraph 25. These are **separate from** (and excluded from) the Enhanced Due Diligence framework for other non-face-to-face onboarding modes, which explicitly applies to “non-face-to-face customer onboarding (other than customer onboarding in terms of paragraph 25)”. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, pp.29-30]; [F.1 Enhanced Due Diligence, pp.45-47]

## 1) Limits to test for breach (and what constitutes a breach)
For accounts opened using **Aadhaar OTP-based e-KYC in non-face-to-face mode**, the bank must ensure the following quantitative caps are not exceeded:

### A. Deposit accounts (aggregate across *all* deposit accounts of the customer)
- **Aggregate balance cap:** “**The aggregate balance of all the deposit accounts of the customer shall not exceed Rupees One Lakh**.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
  - **Breach trigger:** the moment aggregate balance exceeds **₹1,00,000**. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
- **Aggregate credits cap (financial year):** “**The aggregate of all credits in a financial year, in all the deposit accounts taken together, shall not exceed Rupees Two Lakh**.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
  - **Breach trigger:** credits exceeding **₹2,00,000** in the FY (across all deposit accounts taken together). [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

### B. Borrowal accounts
- **Product restriction:** “**As regards borrowal accounts, the bank shall sanction only term loans**.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
- **Aggregate term loan cap (per year):** “**The aggregate amount of term loans sanctioned shall not exceed ₹60,000 in a year**.” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
  - **Breach trigger:** total term loans sanctioned in a year exceeding **₹60,000**. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

### C. Time cap on operating under OTP-based e-KYC without full identification
- “**The bank shall not allow accounts, both deposit and borrowal, opened using OTP based e-KYC to operate for more than one year unless it carries out identification as per paragraph 23 or as per paragraphs 26 and 27 (V-CIP).**” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
  - **Breach trigger:** continuing operations beyond **one year** from opening without completing identification as required. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

## 2) Immediate operational restrictions to apply upon breach (what the bank must do right away)
### A. If the **₹1,00,000 aggregate balance** cap is breached
- The regulation is explicit on the immediate control: “**In case the balance exceeds the threshold, the bank shall cease the account’s operation, until it completes the CDD as mentioned at (6) below.**” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
  - Operationally, this means the bank must **stop operations** on the account(s) until identification/CDD is completed as permitted under paragraph 25(6) (paragraph 23 identification or V-CIP). [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

### B. If the **one-year operating period** is about to end (or has ended) without full identification
- The bank must ensure accounts opened using OTP-based e-KYC **do not operate beyond one year** unless full identification is completed as specified. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

### C. If full identification is **not completed within one year** (explicit consequences)
Paragraph 25(7) prescribes mandatory outcomes:
- “**If the bank does not complete the CDD procedure as mentioned above within a year; (a) in respect of deposit accounts, the bank shall close the same immediately, and (b) in respect of borrowal accounts, the bank shall allow no further debits.**” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
  - **Deposit accounts:** must be **closed immediately**. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
  - **Borrowal accounts:** must permit **“no further debits”**. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

## 3) Deadline for completing “full identification” (CDD) and what it must comprise
### A. Deadline
- The bank must complete identification so that the OTP-based e-KYC account is not allowed “**to operate for more than one year**” without such identification. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

### B. What qualifies as completing identification (CDD) for this purpose
Paragraph 25(6) allows two routes:
- Identification “**as per paragraph 23**”, or [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
- Identification “**as per paragraphs 26 and 27 (V-CIP)**”. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

Under paragraph 23 (individual CDD at onboarding / identification), the bank shall obtain, inter alia:
- Aadhaar number in specified cases / voluntarily, or proof of possession of Aadhaar (with offline verification where possible) or OVD / equivalent e-document, or KYC Identifier with consent to download from CKYCR; **and** [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.25]
- “**PAN or the equivalent e-document thereof or Form No. 60** …” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.25]

Where the bank completes identification using V-CIP with Aadhaar details, paragraph 25(6) further requires: “**If the bank uses Aadhaar details under paragraphs 26 and 27, it shall follow the process in its entirety, including fresh Aadhaar OTP authentication.**” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

## 4) Handling the required customer declaration about not having OTP-based accounts elsewhere
Paragraph 25(8) imposes two compliance actions:

### A. Obtain the customer declaration
- “**The bank shall obtain declaration from the customer to the effect that no other account has been opened nor will be opened using OTP based KYC in non-face-to-face mode with any other RE.**” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

### B. CKYCR upload flagging to prevent reuse by other REs
- “**Further, while uploading KYC information to CKYCR, bank shall clearly indicate that such accounts are opened using OTP based e-KYC and other REs shall not open accounts based on the KYC information of accounts opened with OTP based e-KYC procedure in non-face- to-face mode.**” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

## 5) Additional immediate controls that must be in place for OTP-based e-KYC non-face-to-face accounts (operational guardrails)
Even where no limit is breached yet, paragraph 25 requires the bank to implement the following operational controls (relevant to how you run the account day-to-day):
- **Consent:** “**The Customer shall give specific consent for the authentication through OTP.**” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
- **Transaction alerts/OTPs only to Aadhaar-registered mobile:** the bank must send “**transaction alerts, OTP, etc., only to the mobile number of the customer registered with Aadhaar**”, with a “**Board-approved policy**” for due diligence on mobile number change requests for such accounts. [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.29]
- **Monitoring:** “**The bank shall have strict monitoring procedures including systems to generate alerts in case of any non-compliance / violation, to ensure compliance with the above-mentioned conditions.**” [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]

## What is missing to answer “which limits are breached (if any)” for your case
To determine **which limits are breached in your specific scenario**, the following facts are required but not provided in the query:
- Customer’s **current aggregate balance across all deposit accounts** (to test ₹1,00,000 cap) [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
- Customer’s **aggregate credits in the current financial year across all deposit accounts** (to test ₹2,00,000 cap) [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
- Whether the customer has any **borrowal account**, whether it is a **term loan**, and **aggregate term loans sanctioned in the year** (to test ₹60,000 cap) [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
- The **account opening date** (to compute the one-year operating deadline) [A. CDD Procedure in case of Individuals, p.30]
